title: feat(outbox): aggregateVersion as a first-class field — distinct from event schema version
description:
Checklist review 2026-06-11: DomainEvent.version is the SCHEMA version (defaults 1, for upcasting); the AGGREGATE version is not carried on events or outbox records at all. OutboxRecord is currently { dispatchId, event }. Consumers need aggregateVersion for ordering, idempotency, debugging, replay, and external projections — and the two versions MUST remain distinct fields (never overload one).

Design questions to settle: (a) where it lives — on EventMetadata (stamped by recordEvent? the aggregate knows its version at record time, pre-bump vs post-bump must be pinned — proposal: the version AFTER the mutation that recorded the event, i.e. the value the row will be committed with) vs stamped by withCommit at harvest (then all events of one aggregate share the commit version, simpler, but per-event causality ordering inside one commit is lost); (b) OutboxRecord shape — flattening { id, aggregateId, aggregateType, aggregateVersion, eventType, eventSchemaVersion, payload, occurredAt } is a BREAKING change for every custom Outbox implementer; an additive optional field on the event metadata is non-breaking. Since 1.1.0 is released, prefer the additive path unless a major is planned.

The kit is pre-stable on the UoW feature (Unreleased) but Outbox shipped in 1.x — decide additive-vs-breaking explicitly. Cross-link: projections guide uses last_event_id idempotency; aggregateVersion would strengthen that story.
close_reason: aggregateVersion shipped as optional DomainEvent field, distinct from event.version (schema). Decision: stamped by withCommit at harvest onto frozen copies (commit version = OCC row version; pre-set values via CreateDomainEventOptions win; pendingEvents untouched; Outbox port unchanged — fully additive). Per-commit granularity documented (ES stream positions stay the event store's concern). Contract suite mandatory test asserts committed outbox events carry it; outbox guide recommends indexed columns. 4 new withCommit tests.

title: feat(uow): Phase 1 — explicit-save UnitOfWork core (run API, RepositoryRegistry, lifecycle errors, nesting guard, post-commit marking)
description:
Implement the explicit-save UnitOfWork as a wrapper over withCommit (NOT beside it — outbox handoff, post-commit markPersisted, and best-effort bus publish are inherited). Scope per requirements doc §4/§5/§14/§15/§19/§21-Phase-1:
- UnitOfWork<TContext>.run(work) where the context exposes tx-bound repositories (RepositoryRegistry) + the raw transaction handle. Registry shape per decision bead (enrollment model).
- Context invalidation after close: repository/transaction use after commit or rollback throws TransactionClosedError (§5.4).
- Nesting: forbid in v1 — NestedUnitOfWorkError when run() is called while a UoW is active on the same UnitOfWork instance (§14 Option A; join semantics later).
- Error taxonomy: TransactionClosedError, NestedUnitOfWorkError, CommitError, RollbackError as InfrastructureError subclasses; ConcurrencyConflictError passes through UNCHANGED (§9). Dual-failure: if callback AND rollback fail, throw the primary error with the rollback error attached as cause/secondary (§15).
- Post-commit marking via enrolled aggregates (per decision bead), reusing withCommit's loop semantics (per-aggregate isolation of onPersisted failures).
- Explicit repo.save() only; NO auto-flush, NO savepoints, NO distributed tx (§21).
Tests: requirements doc §23 transaction + post-commit + concurrency + repository-context suites. Docs: new guide section 'Unit of Work' cross-linking withCommit, repository.md partial-writes, and the revised design decision.
design:
API surface fixed by the three decision beads (9or/90w/efe, all decided 2026-06-11):
- UnitOfWork<TCtx>.run(work) wraps withCommit; context = { repositories: <typed registry from user-supplied factory map (tx, session) => repo>, transaction: TCtx }.
- Enrollment: repository registration via UnitOfWorkSession.enrollSaved/enrollDeleted (see ddd-kit-ts-90w design).
- Repo contract: new IUnitOfWorkRepository with delete(aggregate); IRepository untouched (see ddd-kit-ts-efe design).
- design-decisions.md section rewritten ('TransactionScope stays minimal; the Unit of Work lives above it') — keep docs consistent with it.
- Context invalidation scope (be honest in docs + acceptance criteria): enforceable only for what the kit controls — registry getters and session.enroll* throw TransactionClosedError after close; a user holding a raw repo/tx reference past close is a documented contract violation, not a runtime guarantee.
notes:
MikroORM blueprints for this phase (see epic notes for the full analysis):
- Nesting/concurrency guard: #working flag; nested run() inside an active UoW throws NestedUnitOfWorkError (MikroORM: ValidationError.cannotCommit via AsyncLocalStorage insideFlush, uow.ts ~593-617). MikroORM additionally QUEUES concurrent top-level flushes — for v1 we throw on both nested AND concurrent run() on the same instance; a queue can come later if a real use case appears.
- Deterministic close: postCommitCleanup() pattern in a finally block (~1155-1170) — clear enrolled-aggregate set, invalidate the registry/context (subsequent repo/tx use throws TransactionClosedError), reset the active flag. Runs on BOTH commit and rollback paths.
- Single-use semantics: deliberately diverge from MikroORM (EM-scoped, reusable after failed flush) — our run() context is per-operation and dead after close, matching concurrency.md's operation-scoped-aggregates rule.
close_reason: Explicit-save UnitOfWork shipped: run() facade over withCommit, RepositoryFactories registry, UnitOfWorkSession enrollment (saved/deleted, idempotent, save-after-delete throws), error taxonomy (NestedUnitOfWorkError/TransactionClosedError/CommitError/RollbackError/AggregateDeletedError), context invalidation after close, 20 tests, guide + LLM.md + CHANGELOG. Design note: nesting guard is per-instance single-flight; instances are per-operation (parallel-safe by construction).

title: feat(uow): Phase 2 — per-UoW IdentityMap (type+id key, repo integration, deleted-aggregate guard)
description: Ship an IdentityMap implementation (requirements doc §10/§16/§20/§21-Phase-2): get/set/delete/has keyed on aggregateType + id (Restaurant:123 and Booking:123 are distinct entries; key includes the type, never the id alone). Repositories check the map before hydrating and register after loading, so two get(id) calls in one UoW return the SAME instance — this turns the documented Identity Map contract (repository.md, currently the implementer's job) into shipped infrastructure and makes withCommit's reference-dedupe sound by construction. Deleted-aggregate handling: delete(aggregate) marks the entry deleted; a later save() of the same aggregate in the same UoW throws; map cleared when the UoW closes. Tests: §23 identity-map suite. Generic over TId extends Id<string> (no string-typed ids).
notes: MikroORM IdentityMap.ts is the reference implementation (~154 lines, two-level design): Map<AggregateClass, Map<idHash, aggregate>> with lazily created per-type stores (getStore, ~62-73) — cleaner than string-concatenated 'Type:id' keys and gives free per-type iteration. delete() must clean up ALL keys for the instance; MikroORM tracks alternate (business-key) hashes per entity in a WeakMap (#alternateKeys, ~23-53) so cleanup never leaks — adopt the WeakMap pattern if/when findOne-style business-key caching is added (NOT v1). Deleted gate: stricter than MikroORM — re-saving a deleted aggregate in the same UoW THROWS instead of being silently ignored (~518-525 there).
close_reason: IdentityMap shipped: class-keyed two-level storage, strict set (no-op same instance, throws on hydrated-twice violation), deletion tombstones (AggregateDeletedError on re-registration), per-run lifetime with clear-on-close, session.identityMap with TransactionClosedError guard. 13 new tests (9 standalone + 4 UoW integration), guide section, repository.md tip, LLM.md, CHANGELOG.

title: decide(uow): repository surface for UoW — delete(aggregate) vs delete(id), keep Id<Tag> branding
description: Requirements doc §16/§20 wants UnitOfWorkRepository with get(id: string), save(aggregate), delete(aggregate). Two conflicts with the existing kit: (1) IRepository.delete(id) takes an id — the UoW needs the AGGREGATE for event harvest + identity-map removal + deleted-cannot-be-resaved (§16); decide overload vs new interface vs adapter. (2) get(id: string) would lose the Id<Tag> brand — the UoW interfaces MUST stay generic over TId extends Id<string>. Also decide how the existing deletion patterns (repository.md: hard-delete with event harvest via delete(id) inside withCommit) map onto the new surface. Blocks Phase 1.
design:
DECIDED (2026-06-11):
1. IRepository.delete(id) stays UNCHANGED — it serves Pattern-3 hard deletes (GC, no aggregate in hand) and existing consumers.
2. NEW separate interface for UoW-facing repos (name TBD in Phase 1, working name IUnitOfWorkRepository<TAgg extends IAggregateRoot<TId>, TId extends Id<string>>): getById(id: TId), getByIdOrFail(id: TId), save(agg: TAgg), delete(agg: TAgg). delete takes the AGGREGATE because the UoW needs it for (a) pending-event harvest of recorded deletion events, (b) identity-map removal, (c) the deleted-cannot-be-resaved gate; the row deletion itself uses aggregate.id internally.
3. Branded ids EVERYWHERE: TId extends Id<string>; the requirements doc's get(id: string) is rejected — string-typed ids would erase the kit's brand discipline.
4. Concrete repos may implement both interfaces. The documented hard-delete-with-event pattern maps 1:1: order.recordDeletion(reason) then uowRepo.delete(order) — enrollment + harvest replace the manual aggregates-array bookkeeping.
close_reason: Decided and recorded in design fields; Phase 1 (ddd-kit-ts-rdt) design updated with the resulting API surface

title: decide(uow): aggregate enrollment — repository registration vs callback return
description: withCommit gets its aggregates from the callback's RETURN VALUE ({ result, aggregates: [...] }) — a known footgun: forget to return an aggregate and its events never reach the outbox, silently. The requirements doc (§19) has repositories REGISTER saved aggregates with the UoW instead; with a RepositoryRegistry + IdentityMap this falls out naturally and is structurally safer (save() enrolls; impossible to forget). Decide: (a) repo-registration as the UoW model with withCommit's return-array kept for standalone use; (b) how delete() enrolls (events harvested, aggregate marked deleted in identity map); (c) whether read-only loads enroll (no — only save/delete). The decision shapes the UnitOfWorkRepository interface, so it blocks Phase 1.
design:
DECIDED (2026-06-11): REPOSITORY REGISTRATION.
- The UoW hands repository factories a session handle alongside the tx: UnitOfWorkSession { enrollSaved(agg): void; enrollDeleted(agg): void }.
- Repos call enrollSaved(aggregate) at the end of save(); delete(aggregate) calls enrollDeleted(aggregate). Enrollment is idempotent per instance (Set semantics, mirrors withCommit's reference dedupe).
- run() passes the enrolled set to withCommit as its aggregates array — deleted aggregates included, so their recorded deletion events are harvested into the outbox; the post-commit markPersisted on a deleted instance is harmless (caller discards the reference, same as today's documented Pattern 2).
- Read paths do NOT enroll (Phase 2: they only register in the identity map).
- RATIONALE: the footgun moves from per-call-site (every use case must remember the aggregates array) to per-implementation (each repo calls enroll once, verified once by that repo's tests). MikroORM uses the same model (persist/remove stacks fed by API calls, not by callback returns).
- withCommit's return-array path stays fully supported for standalone (non-UoW) use.
notes: MikroORM reference: separate #persistStack / #removeStack Sets keyed by instance (uow.ts ~47-50) with remove() evicting from the persist stack (~518-525) — the registration-based model this decision leans toward is exactly MikroORM's, minus cascades (aggregates have no relation metadata; the aggregate boundary IS the cascade boundary). Repos' save()/delete() enroll instances into UoW-internal stacks; the UoW never attaches tracking state to the aggregate itself.
close_reason: Decided and recorded in design fields; Phase 1 (ddd-kit-ts-rdt) design updated with the resulting API surface

title: decide(uow): revise the 'no Fowler UoW' stance in design-decisions.md
description: docs/guide/design-decisions.md currently documents 'TransactionScope (not Fowler UoW: no change tracking)' as a deliberate decision. The UoW epic (ddd-kit-ts-nnr) builds exactly a Fowler-style UoW in phases — legitimate evolution now that AggregateRoot has dirty tracking (changedKeys/hasChanges, shipped 2026-06-11), but the documented decision must be consciously revised, not silently contradicted. Deliverable: rewrite the design-decisions.md entry explaining WHY the stance changed (dirty tracking made the textbook full-aggregate repository affordable; the UoW facade removes the enrollment footgun) and what stays true (TransactionScope remains the low-level port; withCommit remains usable standalone).
close_reason: design-decisions.md section rewritten: TransactionScope stays minimal, change detection on the aggregate, orchestration in withCommit, opt-in UoW facade as planned next layer

title: epic(uow): UnitOfWork facade over withCommit — explicit-save UoW with repository registry, identity map, error taxonomy
description:
Build a UnitOfWork layer per the requirements doc (2026-06-11 session). KEY INSIGHT FROM GAP ANALYSIS: a large part is ALREADY SHIPPED — withCommit + TransactionScope cover transaction lifecycle (§5), post-commit aggregate lifecycle (§7: markPersisted only after commit, never on rollback, events cleared only at markPersisted), OCC propagation (§9: ConcurrencyConflictError distinguishable, retryable), outbox-in-same-tx + post-commit publish (§13), and the multi-table save pattern with changedKeys/hasChanges dirty tracking (§8/§18, built + review-hardened 2026-06-11: hasChanges covers version-only changes, pendingEvents, keyless states). The UoW MUST be built as a wrapper over withCommit, not beside it — requirements-doc Phase 3 (outbox) is therefore already done.

GENUINELY NEW: (1) UnitOfWork<TContext>.run() facade handing the callback tx-bound repositories via a RepositoryRegistry (§4/§20) instead of the raw tx; (2) shipped IdentityMap implementation keyed type+id (§10); (3) nesting guard NestedUnitOfWorkError (§14, Option A: forbid first, join later); (4) error taxonomy TransactionClosedError/CommitError/RollbackError + dual-failure handling preserving the primary error, context invalidation after close (§5.4/§15); (5) delete(aggregate) semantics + deleted-cannot-be-resaved (§16); (6) optional read-only mode (§17); (7) optional auto-flush LAST (§12/§24: explicit save first).

Blocked by three design decisions (separate beads) and sequenced in phase beads. Acceptance criteria: requirements doc §22; required tests: §23.
notes:
INSPIRATION (user-provided, battle-tested): MikroORM UnitOfWork — github.com/mikro-orm/mikro-orm/blob/master/packages/core/src/unit-of-work/UnitOfWork.ts (analyzed 2026-06-11, ~1725 lines, plus ChangeSet.ts + IdentityMap.ts).

ADOPT (fits the kit's philosophy):
1. Double-flush guard: #working flag; concurrent commit() calls QUEUE and run sequentially; a flush triggered INSIDE a flush hook throws (ValidationError.cannotCommit, uow.ts ~593-617). Direct blueprint for NestedUnitOfWorkError + the run()-active guard.
2. postCommitCleanup() in a finally block (~1155-1170): clears all stacks + working flag deterministically — blueprint for our context-invalidation-after-close.
3. Two-level IdentityMap: Map<EntityClass, Map<idHash, entity>> with per-type lazy stores (IdentityMap.ts ~6-73) — exactly our type+id keying. Alternate-key hashes (business-key lookups) tracked in a WeakMap for cleanup — optional later, note for Phase 2.
4. Separate persist/remove stacks with a one-way deleted gate: remove() drops the entity from the persist stack; a managed+removed entity is never resurrected (~518-589). We go STRICTER: re-saving a deleted aggregate THROWS (explicit > MikroORM's silent ignore).
5. UoW-internal tracking state in Maps/Sets keyed by instance (never hidden __props on the tracked object) — matches our frozen-state aggregates.

REJECT (does not fit):
- Proxy/metadata-driven dirty tracking → our aggregate-level changedKeys/hasChanges shallow reference diff is the kit's answer.
- Auto-flush before queries (~386-401) → explicit save, v1 has no auto-flush at all.
- Metadata-driven cascades + automatic inverse-relation cleanup → aggregates own their consistency boundary; hand-written repos, no relation metadata.
- Hidden wrapper props (__managed/__originalEntityData) on entities → frozen plain-record states forbid it anyway.
- Payload-mutating flush hooks → withCommit's harvest is our only 'onFlush'; events are deeply frozen.

CONCURRENCY DIFFERENCE (keep ours): MikroORM checks the version pre-flush against the in-memory snapshot (lockOptimistic ~1264-1288); the kit checks at the DB row (UPDATE ... WHERE version = persistedVersion) — stronger, actual-DB-state check. No change.

SCOPE DIFFERENCE: MikroORM's UoW is EntityManager-scoped and reusable after a failed flush (stacks survive). Ours is run()-scoped and single-use (TransactionClosedError after close) — simpler and matches the operation-scoped-aggregates rule in concurrency.md.

CHILDREN
  ↳ ✓ ddd-kit-ts-syz: feat(uow): Phase 2 — per-UoW IdentityMap (type+id key, repo integration, deleted-aggregate guard) ● P2
  ↳ ○ ddd-kit-ts-las: decide(repo): DuplicateAggregateError / constraint-violation mapping ● P3
  ↳ ✓ ddd-kit-ts-rdt: feat(uow): Phase 1 — explicit-save UnitOfWork core (run API, RepositoryRegistry, lifecycle errors, nesting guard, post-commit marking) ● P2
  ↳ ✓ ddd-kit-ts-efe: decide(uow): repository surface for UoW — delete(aggregate) vs delete(id), keep Id<Tag> branding ● P2
  ↳ ○ ddd-kit-ts-7am: feat(outbox): aggregateVersion as a first-class field — distinct from event schema version ● P2
  ↳ ○ ddd-kit-ts-nc8: feat(uow): Phase 4 (optional) — auto-flush ● P4
  ↳ ✓ ddd-kit-ts-90w: decide(uow): aggregate enrollment — repository registration vs callback return ● P2
  ↳ ○ ddd-kit-ts-hox: feat(testing): Repository Contract Test Suite — RELEASE BLOCKER for any stable/public UoW claim ● P1
  ↳ ○ ddd-kit-ts-kc5: feat(uow): Phase 3 (optional) — read-only UoW mode ● P3
  ↳ ✓ ddd-kit-ts-9or: decide(uow): revise the 'no Fowler UoW' stance in design-decisions.md ● P2
  ◐ 5/10 complete (50%)

RELEASE GATE (checklist review 2026-06-11, user decision): the UoW/OCC feature must NOT be claimed stable/public until the Repository Contract Test Suite (ddd-kit-ts-hox, P1) ships — OCC is a repository CONTRACT the kit must make testable, not a documented pattern. Hard acceptance phrasing: 'Die UoW-Boundary erfüllt die Mindestdefinition strukturell. Die OCC-Garantie ist aber nur erfüllt, wenn jedes Consumer-Repository das Version-Predicate korrekt implementiert und die Contract-Tests besteht.'

title: feat(aggregate): dirty-tracking on AggregateRoot — changedKeys() since restore (unit-of-work write support)
description:
Repositories for aggregates whose state spans multiple tables (root row + N child-collection tables) currently have two bad options on save: write everything (write amplification), or have the application service orchestrate per-collection writes manually — which reopens the door to forgotten OCC version bumps and per-step boilerplate.

MOTIVATING ANTI-PATTERN (observed in a real consumer codebase): an aggregate keeps its child collections OUT of aggregate state because a full-aggregate save couldn't know which collections changed. Consequences cascade: collection writes bypass the aggregate, the OCC version no longer bumps by construction, and a domain-level "touch" method exists solely to advance the version manually — opt-in per application-service method, silently forgettable (a forgotten call compiles, passes single-writer tests, and saves successfully while staying invisible to every other instance's OCC check; the consumer had to add a hand-written assertion in its save path to catch it). Library-level dirty tracking dissolves that whole workaround and makes the textbook full-aggregate repository affordable.

WHY IT'S CHEAP IN DDD-KIT (unlike Hibernate-style proxy/deep-diff dirty checking): setState() replaces state immutably and _state is freezeShallow'd. Unchanged top-level sub-objects therefore KEEP REFERENCE IDENTITY across mutations — a shallow per-key !== comparison between the current state and the state at restore time is an EXACT dirty check, O(top-level keys), no proxies, no deep diff, no setState bookkeeping.

Anatomy fit (current src/aggregate/*): BaseAggregate owns version/persistedVersion/markRestored/markPersisted but no state; Entity owns _state + freezeShallow; AggregateRoot joins both — so AggregateRoot is the layer that can snapshot the state reference at the persistence-lifecycle markers and diff lazily on demand.

Scope: AggregateRoot only. EventSourcedAggregate doesn't need it — its pendingEvents ARE the change record.
close_reason: Implemented changedKeys/hasChanges dirty tracking on AggregateRoot with docs and 23 new tests; all gates green

title: assertSnapshotSafe: spoofed-tag bypass and symbol-key false negative
description: src/aggregate/base-aggregate.ts:394/409 — (a) a plain object with Symbol.toStringTag='Date' matches SNAPSHOT_SAFE_TAGS and is skipped, so nested functions escape the fail-fast walk and die later as pathless DataCloneError (fix: brand-verify via isBuiltInObject); (b) symbol-keyed state passes the guard but structuredClone silently drops it (fix: flag symbol keys as snapshot-unsafe); (c) Reflect.ownKeys also walks non-enumerable props structuredClone ignores (stricter than reality, fails closed — acceptable but document). Both VERIFIED. Found by code review of fix branch.
close_reason: Fixed: brand-verified guard, Error + symbol-key fail-fast, structuredClone-aligned walk; 5 new tests

title: shadowMutators crashes on sealed/non-extensible (not frozen) Date
description: src/value-object/value-object.ts:50 — guard checks Object.isFrozen only; a sealed Date with a writable expando is not frozen but non-extensible, so Object.defineProperty(obj,'setTime',...) throws 'object is not extensible'. Regression vs main (deepFreeze handled sealed Dates fine). VERIFIED. Fix: guard on !Object.isExtensible(obj). Found by code review of fix branch.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/p1-review-fixes; covered by 12 new tests

title: SNAPSHOT_SAFE_TAGS whitelists Error: subclasses silently downgraded in snapshots
description: src/aggregate/base-aggregate.ts:324 — custom Error subclasses in aggregate state pass the class-instance check ([object Error] tag) and structuredClone silently downgrades them to plain Error (instanceof broken, custom fields partially lost) — the exact silent corruption assertSnapshotSafe exists to catch. VERIFIED. Decide: treat Error in state as snapshot-unsafe (fail fast) or document. Found by code review of fix branch.
close_reason: Fixed: brand-verified guard, Error + symbol-key fail-fast, structuredClone-aligned walk; 5 new tests

title: vo() silently drops symbol-keyed properties; existing test passes vacuously
description: src/value-object/value-object.ts:62 — structuredClone does not clone symbol keys, so vo({ [Symbol('tag')]: { nested: 1 } }) silently loses the property. The existing test vo.test.ts:508-516 ('freezes properties whose key is a Symbol') passes vacuously — v[tag] is undefined and Object.isFrozen(undefined) === true. The deepFreeze docstring's claimed symbol-key symmetry with deepEqual is unreachable through vo(). Either preserve symbol keys or document+throw.
close_reason: Fixed: cloneForVo walks plain structures preserving symbol keys; vacuous test made strict; 4 tests

title: deepFreeze immutability guarantee broken for Date/Map/Set internals
description: src/value-object/value-object.ts:42 and src/aggregate/domain-event.ts:406 — Object.freeze does not protect internal slots: event.occurredAt.setTime(0) succeeds on a frozen event, and vo({ m: new Map() }).m.set(...) succeeds on a frozen VO. A mutating subscriber can poison occurredAt for all subsequent handlers — exactly the scenario the deep-freeze comment (domain-event.ts:403-405) claims to prevent. Additionally, when the caller passes options.occurredAt, the event shares the caller's live Date instance.
close_reason: Fixed: mutator shadowing for Date/Map/Set + recursive Map/Set content freeze + defensive occurredAt copy; 9 new tests

title: deepEqual/deepOmit: Symbol.toStringTag spoofing causes TypeError crashes
description: src/utils/array/deep-equal.ts:147-178 (and deep-omit.ts:165 via cloneBuiltIn) — type detection is purely tag-based, then type-specific methods are called without verifying the object actually is that type. Trigger: deepEqual({ [Symbol.toStringTag]: 'Date' }, { [Symbol.toStringTag]: 'Date' }) → TypeError: objA.getTime is not a function; with 'Map' → TypeError: mapA is not iterable. Plain objects setting a toStringTag are common in libraries and crash equality checks.
close_reason: Fixed: internal-slot brand probes in isBuiltInObject + Array.isArray routing in deepEqual/deepOmit; 11 new tests

title: deepOmit: visited cache conflates DAG sharing with cycles, reusing path-sensitive predicate results
description: src/utils/array/deep-omit.ts:73-75 — when the same object appears under two paths, the ignoreKeyPredicate result computed for the first path is wrongly reused for the second. Trigger: const s = { x: 1, y: 2 }; deepOmit({ a: s, b: s }, { ignoreKeyPredicate: (k, p) => k === 'x' && p[0] === 'a' }) → result.b.x is undefined even though the predicate only ignores x under 'a'. Cycle detection needs to be separated from result memoization (or memoize per path).
close_reason: Fixed: ancestor-only cache in path-sensitive mode; 3 new tests (per-path predicate, cycle termination, dedup regression guard)

title: createSnapshot: structuredClone strips prototypes of class-based child entities
description: src/aggregate/base-aggregate.ts:223 (also aggregate-root.ts:169, event-sourced-aggregate.ts:183) — createSnapshot() → structuredClone(this._state) clones class-based child entities (the documented pattern in entity.ts header: OrderState contains OrderItem extends EntityBase) as plain objects, so restoreFromSnapshot/restoreFromSnapshotWithEvents reconstitutes an aggregate whose child entities crash on first method call (item.calculateSubtotal is not a function) — long after the snapshot round-trip appeared to succeed. If state holds a function-valued property, createSnapshot throws DataCloneError outright.
close_reason: Fixed per B+C design: fail-fast plain-data check + toSnapshotState/fromSnapshotState hooks + TSnapshotState generic; 6 new tests; guide + LLM.md updated

title: ValueObject ctor and Entity freeze caller-owned objects in place
description: src/value-object/value-object.ts:252 — deepFreeze({ ...props }) only shallow-copies; nested objects are frozen by reference, mutating the caller's object graph (the very side effect vo() documents and avoids via structuredClone). Trigger: const meta = { a: 1 }; new MyVO({ meta }); Object.isFrozen(meta) === true. Similarly src/entity/entity.ts:158 and :196 — freezeShallow(initialState) freezes the very object the caller passed; a later write to it throws TypeError in strict mode. Fix: copy before freezing.
close_reason: Fixed: ValueObject deep-clones props via deepOmit before freeze; Entity shallow-copies plain-object/array state before freezeShallow; 5 new tests

title: EventSourcedAggregate: handler lookup walks the prototype chain
description: src/aggregate/event-sourced-aggregate.ts:121-128 — this.handlers[event.type] is a plain property get on an object literal, so for event.type === 'toString' it returns Object.prototype.toString, which passes the !handler check and is invoked as a handler (_state becomes '[object Undefined]'). 'constructor' silently no-ops; '__proto__'/'hasOwnProperty' crash with a raw TypeError instead of MissingHandlerError. loadFromHistory is documented as the boundary where stream corruption surfaces as a recoverable failure, but these type strings bypass both the MissingHandlerError path and the DomainError→err conversion. Fix: Object.hasOwn(this.handlers, event.type) or a null-prototype handlers map.
close_reason: Fixed: Object.hasOwn guard in dispatchAndCommit; 6 new tests covering toString/constructor/__proto__/hasOwnProperty/valueOf + loadFromHistory

title: loadFromHistory leaves aggregate partially mutated on mid-stream DomainError
description: src/aggregate/event-sourced-aggregate.ts:149-163 — each dispatchAndCommit assigns this._state immediately, but markRestored only runs on success. agg.loadFromHistory([validEvent, invalidEvent]) returns err while _state already reflects validEvent and version was never advanced — state and version are mutually inconsistent; reusing or re-loading the aggregate produces wrong results. Sibling restoreFromSnapshotWithEvents (lines 179-194) explicitly snapshots previousState/previousVersion and rolls back in the catch; loadFromHistory has no equivalent. No test covers aggregate state after a failed loadFromHistory.
close_reason: Fixed: pre-call state snapshot + rollback in catch; 2 new tests (DomainError and non-domain propagation)

title: Adopt @shirudo/base-error v5: validation aggregate + RFC 9457 glue
description:
base-error v5 ships ValidationError (StructuredError-based, collects N field issues, ingests Standard Schema output, serializes to RFC 9457 via toProblemDetails, safe-by-default). This replaces the previously-planned hand-built validation aggregate (would have duplicated upstream).

Scope (Option A — adopt + thin DDD glue):
1. Bump peer/dev dep @shirudo/base-error ^4.7.0 -> ^5.0.0 and run smoke test (vitest run + tsc --noEmit). Verify class-based error tests still pass (e.name / toJSON().name / isBaseError — BaseError name+_tag inference is preserved in v5; only StructuredError._tag changed).
2. Add thin VO/Result glue: voValidated(t, rules) -> Result<VO<T>, ValidationError> using base-error's ValidationError; collect multiple field issues before returning err.
3. Re-export ValidationError (and relevant types) from ddd-kit so consumers get RFC 9457 + Standard Schema for free.
4. Docs: update errors.ts:12 (kit now ships one generic concrete error via re-export) and result-vs-throw.md:130; add validation guide page.

Decision recorded: ValidationError is a StructuredError (carries code/category) — the first such in the kit, a deliberate exception because validation->RFC 9457 is exactly where structured fields are wanted.
notes:
Step 4 DONE — architecture-review-driven refinements applied (kit-not-framework):

title: ddd-kit docs: document someChainRetryable usage for retryable chain checks
description:
Adopt the upstream someChainRetryable helper (see dependency bead) in ddd-kit's docs and common-mistakes guidance so consumers don't reinvent the workaround comment about isChainRetryable's StructuredError-shape filter.

Three doc additions, all in @shirudo/ddd-kit:

1. docs/guide/result-vs-throw.md — under the existing isRetryable section, add a short table or paragraph distinguishing the @shirudo/base-error helpers that work with ddd-kit errors (loose: isRetryable, someCauseChain, findInCauseChain, getRootCause) from those that don't (strict StructuredError-only: isChainRetryable, isRetryableStructuredError, getRootCauseRetryable, getFirstRetryableCause). Show the correct chain-retryable composition: someChainRetryable(err) (or someCauseChain(err, isRetryable) if the consumer is on a pre-someChainRetryable base-error version).

2. docs/guide/repository.md — in the existing ConcurrencyConflictError example, change the recommended pattern from isRetryable(err) (single-level) to someChainRetryable(err) (whole chain) so consumers walking wrapped errors get correct behaviour.

3. llms.txt Common Mistakes block, silent-runtime bucket — new entry:
   'Using isChainRetryable(err) on a wrapped ddd-kit error (ConcurrencyConflictError in the cause chain). It returns false silently because isChainRetryable filters on StructuredError shape; ddd-kit errors extend BaseError directly without code/category. Use someChainRetryable(err) (or someCauseChain(err, isRetryable) on older base-error versions).'

Optional follow-up (own bead if pursued): a one-line ddd-kit re-export so consumers don't need to know which base-error subset works — but this couples ddd-kit's API to base-error internals and is best deferred unless real consumer demand emerges.

Acceptance:
  1. result-vs-throw.md has the helper-compatibility table or paragraph
  2. repository.md example updated to someChainRetryable (with fallback note for older base-error)
  3. llms.txt Common Mistakes has the new entry under 'Silent runtime bugs'
  4. CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased] as Documentation, non-breaking

CHANGELOG (Unreleased/Documentation), docs/guide/result-vs-throw.md (catch example + compatibility callout), docs/guide/repository.md (OCC example + tip callout), llms.txt (silent-runtime entry + helper list). Peer-dep range left at ^4.6.0; pre-4.7 fallback (someCauseChain + isRetryable) documented inline. 441/441 tests pass; vitepress build clean.

title: Upstream: add someChainRetryable helper to @shirudo/base-error
description:
Add a new helper function to @shirudo/base-error that composes the existing someCauseChain + isRetryable into a single convenience entry, parallel to the existing isChainRetryable but without the strict StructuredError-shape requirement.

Problem (verified against base-error@4.6.0 source):

  isChainRetryable(err)
    -> findInCauseChain(err, isRetryableStructuredError)
    -> isRetryableStructuredError(e) = isStructuredError(e) && e.retryable === true
    -> isStructuredError(e) requires code: string + category: string + retryable: boolean

This means any BaseError subclass that sets retryable: true but has no code/category fields is silently filtered out by isChainRetryable — including everything in ddd-kit's error hierarchy (DomainError / InfrastructureError / ConcurrencyConflictError / AggregateNotFoundError / MissingHandlerError, all extending BaseError directly per the documented BaseError contract).

The fix is additive and one-line:

  // src/traversal/retryability.ts
  export function someChainRetryable(error: unknown, maxDepth = 100): boolean {
    return someCauseChain(error, isRetryable, maxDepth);
  }

Rationale for additive (NOT changing isChainRetryable in place):

  - Zero breaking impact on existing isChainRetryable consumers (StructuredError-only is a legitimate strict-mode pattern for RFC-9457 service-layer errors).
  - Clear intent differentiation in the API surface: 'is any retryable' (loose, BaseError-compatible) vs 'is any retryable StructuredError' (strict, structured-only). Both legitimate questions.
  - JS-idiomatic naming: 'some' parallels Array.some semantics that callers already know.

Acceptance:

  1. New function someChainRetryable in src/traversal/retryability.ts that composes someCauseChain + isRetryable
  2. JSDoc explaining the difference vs isChainRetryable, with a worked example using a BaseError subclass with retryable: true
  3. Export from package index alongside isChainRetryable, getRootCauseRetryable, getFirstRetryableCause
  4. Unit tests covering: returns true for direct retryable BaseError, returns true for wrapped retryable BaseError, returns false for non-retryable chain, respects maxDepth, matches semantics of someCauseChain(err, isRetryable)
  5. Minor doc note in README contrasting the two helpers (strict StructuredError vs loose BaseError-with-retryable)

Repo: @shirudo/base-error (separate repository from ddd-kit). Bead lives here to track the dependency for the ddd-kit doc-fix that uses the new helper.
notes: Upstream PR opened: https://github.com/shi-rudo/base-error-ts/pull/17 (branch feat/some-chain-retryable). Adds someChainRetryable composing someCauseChain over loose isRetryable predicate. 9 new tests, 212 total passing, lint+tsc clean. Closes when PR merges & published to npm; then o5z (downstream docs) unblocks.
close_reason: Upstream PR #17 merged and published as @shirudo/base-error@4.7.0. Verified someChainRetryable present in npm tarball (dist/index.d.ts, dist/index.js).

title: recordEvent helper + withCommit aggregate-metadata validation
description:
Two-part hardening of aggregate-emitted event identity. Today aggregateId and aggregateType on DomainEvent are optional; aggregate events that forget them silently break downstream consumers (outbox dispatchers, projection handlers, audit logs). Per consumer-feedback discussion: optionality at the type level was a footgun; doc-recommendation alone won't fix it.

Scope:

1. Add abstract instance property on both aggregate flavours:
   protected abstract readonly aggregateType: string

   This is compile-time enforcement: every subclass must declare its
   aggregate type as a string literal at the class level. No
   constructor.name fragility, no static-property dance.

2. Add protected recordEvent(type, payload, options?) on both
   AggregateRoot and EventSourcedAggregate. It calls createDomainEvent
   with aggregateId = this.id and aggregateType = this.aggregateType
   auto-injected. options must NOT contain aggregateId / aggregateType
   (type-omits them).

3. Add a runtime guard in withCommit that validates every harvested
   event has BOTH aggregateId AND aggregateType set. Throws a
   descriptive error naming the event.type and the missing fields when
   a direct createDomainEvent call bypassed recordEvent inside an
   aggregate.

BREAKING for consumers because the abstract property requires every
existing Aggregate subclass to declare aggregateType. Acceptable
in rc.x because:
  - Single one-line addition per Aggregate subclass
  - Catches a real category of footgun (missing routing metadata)
  - Aligns with Axon / EventFlow which require it via annotation
  - Migration is trivial: protected readonly aggregateType = "Order"

Future direction (NOT in this bead, just noted):
The cleaner long-term shape is splitting DomainEvent into a base
shape and a richer AggregateDomainEvent that REQUIRES aggregateId
and aggregateType at the type level. AggregateRoot's TEvent would be
constrained to AggregateDomainEvent. That would eliminate the
runtime guard entirely. Consider for 2.0.

Acceptance:
  - AggregateRoot and EventSourcedAggregate both expose
    protected abstract readonly aggregateType: string
  - Both expose protected recordEvent<E extends TEvent>(type, payload, options?)
    that auto-injects aggregateId (this.id) and aggregateType (this.aggregateType)
  - withCommit validates every harvested event has aggregateId AND
    aggregateType; throws with a message naming the event type and
    listing missing fields
  - All in-kit examples migrated: examples/order/, examples/order-with-entity-items/,
    examples/rugby/, examples/saga/ (Order, Payment, Shipment, CheckoutSaga)
  - Tests added: recordEvent injects both fields, recordEvent value
    survives through withCommit, guard throws with correct message on
    missing aggregateId, guard throws on missing aggregateType, direct
    createDomainEvent outside an aggregate still works
  - CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased] as BREAKING
  - JSDoc on the existing createDomainEvent recommends recordEvent for
    aggregate-internal use
  - llms.txt Common Mistakes block gets an entry: 'Calling
    createDomainEvent from inside an aggregate domain method →
    withCommit guard rejects the missing aggregateId/Type at runtime.
    Use this.recordEvent(type, payload) instead.'
close_reason: Closed

title: llms.txt: add TEvent=never warning, Common Mistakes section, rc.6 renames, @next install note
description:
Post-rc.7 review of llms.txt surfaced four real gaps. Doc-only, llms.txt only.

1. TEvent=never default not warned about — real footgun. AggregateRoot<TState, TId, TEvent> in the API-surface section makes TEvent look optional and harmless. Since rc.4, TEvent defaults to 'never', so an aggregate that emits events without declaring TEvent gets 'argument not assignable to never' on every addDomainEvent. Add a principle line near the aggregate-class bullet.

2. No consolidated 'Common Mistakes / LLM Footguns' section. Footguns are scattered across the principles and API-surface; LLMs benefit from a consolidated block they parse as a unit. Six lines covering: markPersisted override, pendingEvents rename, save(tx, agg) wrong shape, TEvent missing, module-level aggregate instances on Workers, mocking CommandBus in unit tests.

3. rc.6 renames not migration-explicit. domainEvents -> pendingEvents and clearDomainEvents() -> clearPendingEvents() are documented as 'on the interface' but the old names appear in LLM training data and pre-rc.6 snippets. Add a short renames-list near the version header so LLMs translating older snippets see the swap.

4. Minor: install note pins to default 'latest' dist-tag. RC releases live under 'next'. Change install snippet to pnpm add @shirudo/ddd-kit@next ... so LLMs follow consumers will land on rc.7 actually.
close_reason: Closed

title: Document Identity-Map requirement for Repository implementations
description:
The withCommit aggregate-dedupe (ddd-kit-ts-0wf, just landed) relies on JavaScript object identity — two getById(id) calls within the same Unit of Work must return the SAME in-memory instance for the dedupe to be conceptually sound. This is Fowler's Identity Map pattern (PoEAA, 2002), which Evans, Vernon, Khononov, and Young all implicitly assume but the kit's docs don't state.

Why this is a real gap: a consumer reads withCommit's JSDoc, sees 'dedupes by object identity', and either:

1. Assumes the kit handles aggregate identity entirely (wrong — only ref-identity, not id-identity), OR
2. Builds a Repository without identity-map semantics. Two separate getById(id) calls return two distinct instances with same id. withCommit's dedupe doesn't catch this. Same-aggregate logical duplicate slides through. Outbox double-emits. Silent.

The kit's stance is permissive (convention, not enforcement) which is consistent with its 'ship parts, not framework' philosophy. But the convention has to be STATED — otherwise consumers don't know to follow it.

Acceptance:

1. New short section in docs/guide/repository.md (under the existing 'IRepository — id-canonical access' header, after the getById/getByIdOrFail discussion). Heading: 'Identity Map: one instance per aggregate per Unit of Work'.

2. Content covers:
   - Fowler's Identity Map pattern referenced by name (PoEAA, 2002)
   - The contract: 'two getById(id) calls within the same transactional scope (the same withCommit invocation) MUST return the same in-memory instance'
   - Why: withCommit's aggregate-dedupe assumes ref-identity == aggregate-identity; this contract makes that assumption true
   - How: most ORM identity maps (Drizzle session, Prisma client, EF Core context) provide this for free; a hand-rolled Map<TId, TAgg> per-tx works too
   - The negative case: if your repository returns distinct instances for the same id within one UoW, withCommit's dedupe will not catch the duplicate. Two events from the 'same' aggregate land in the outbox; markPersisted runs twice on two different instances. Silent corruption.

3. Brief mention in withCommit's JSDoc cross-referencing this section, so a reader landing on withCommit sees the dependency.

4. CHANGELOG entry as Documentation under [Unreleased].
close_reason: Closed

title: Scoped factory helpers: withEventIdFactory / withClockFactory
description:
setEventIdFactory and setClockFactory in src/aggregate/domain-event.ts mutate module-level globals (currentEventIdFactory, currentClockFactory). This causes real race conditions in two consumer scenarios:

1. **Parallel tests** — vitest runs with pool: 'threads' (or 'forks') by default. Test A calls setEventIdFactory(deterministicGen), Test B calls createDomainEvent(...) concurrently and reads A's factory. The 'call once at bootstrap' guidance in the JSDoc breaks down for tests that explicitly want per-test factories.

2. **Multi-tenant servers** — Request A wants tenant-specific clock or id-gen, Request B is in flight; globals collide across requests sharing the same process.

Fix: ship scoped helper functions that save the current factory, install the new one, run the callback, restore. Synchronous-only (the closure must complete before the original factory is restored).

API:
- withEventIdFactory<T>(factory: EventIdFactory, fn: () => T): T
- withClockFactory<T>(factory: ClockFactory, fn: () => T): T

Both wrap their body in try/finally so the restoration runs even on throw. Additive, non-breaking — the existing setEventIdFactory / setClockFactory stay as global-set helpers; the new helpers are for scope-limited usage.

Async-scoped factories (AsyncLocalStorage-based for spanning awaits) is a separate, edge-runtime-conditional concern and is out of scope for this bead. The sync version covers tests and synchronous code paths within a request handler.

Acceptance:
1. Two new exported helpers in src/aggregate/domain-event.ts: withEventIdFactory and withClockFactory.
2. Each uses try/finally to restore the previous factory.
3. Tests in domain-event.test.ts covering: factory is installed during fn, restored after fn returns, restored after fn throws, nested calls compose correctly (inner restore returns to outer's factory, then outer restore returns to original).
4. JSDoc on the existing setEventIdFactory / setClockFactory points at the scoped helpers as the safer alternative for tests and short-lived contexts.
5. CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased] as Added, non-breaking.
close_reason: Closed

title: Document aggregate reconstitution pattern (state-stored + ES)
description:
Vernon §11 / Khononov / Young all distinguish two construction paths: factory (for new aggregates, records creation events) vs reconstitution / rehydration / materialization (for loading existing aggregates from persistence, no events). The kit ships the mechanisms for both but only documents one half:

  - Event-sourced aggregates: loadFromHistory(events) IS the reconstitution path; documented in event-sourcing.md.
  - State-stored aggregates: no documented pattern. The Repository implementor has to figure out that 'protected constructor + protected setVersion' is the kit's reconstitution surface — new Order(id, state) + order.setVersion(version) from inside the aggregate class via a static reconstitute(id, state, version) helper. The pattern works with the existing API but is invisible.

This bead is Option A from the user-facing discussion: documentation-only, no API change. The pattern is 2 lines of code; a helper would be overkill and TS-generics on static methods with -typing get ugly.

Acceptance:

1. New section in docs/guide/aggregates.md after the 'Construction: static factory methods' subsection, titled 'Reconstitution: loading existing aggregates from persistence'. Covers:
   - The distinction (Vernon §11): factory for new, reconstitution for existing. Cite Vernon / Khononov / Young terminology variations (reconstitute / materialize / rehydrate).
   - State-stored aggregates: static Order.reconstitute(id, state, version) calling protected constructor + protected setVersion. Worked example.
   - Event-sourced aggregates: loadFromHistory IS the reconstitution path; cross-link to event-sourcing.md.
   - Explicit note that reconstitution records NO events — the aggregate already exists in the world; reconstitution is purely an in-memory assembly step.

2. Update docs/guide/repository.md to show a Repository.getById implementation calling Order.reconstitute(row.id, row.state, row.version) so consumers see the call pattern in the persistence context.

3. CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased] as Added. Non-breaking — pure documentation.
close_reason: Closed

title: Document version=0 insert vs version>0 update convention in IRepository.save
description:
src/repo/repository.ts:46-66 IRepository.save JSDoc says the impl handles 'insert or update' but never tells the implementor how to distinguish. The standard convention is:
  - aggregate.version === 0 -> INSERT (fresh aggregate, never persisted)
  - aggregate.version >  0 -> UPDATE (with OCC: WHERE id=? AND version=?)

Every Drizzle/Prisma/Mongo adapter has to reinvent this. Worth documenting once and pointing at from impl-side docs.

Acceptance:
- One paragraph added to IRepository.save's JSDoc (src/repo/repository.ts) stating the convention
- One paragraph in docs/guide/repository.md, same statement plus a code snippet showing both paths in a Drizzle-flavoured impl (INSERT for v=0, UPDATE...WHERE version=expected for v>0; throw ConcurrencyConflictError on row count = 0 for the update)
- No code change required
close_reason: Closed

title: Document CQRS read-side projection flow (outbox -> projection -> QueryBus)
description:
The library has all pieces for canonical CQRS: Outbox for durable event handoff, QueryBus for reads, EventBus for in-process subscribers. But the standard 'outbox-dispatcher feeds projection-updaters which populate read-model tables which QueryBus reads from' is documented nowhere end-to-end.

docs/guide/cqrs-and-buses.md covers Command + Query + withCommit but stops short of the read-model loop. docs/guide/outbox.md covers add/getPending/markDispatched in isolation. The piece that ties them together is missing.

Acceptance: one of
A) New section in docs/guide/outbox.md: 'Read-side projections' showing pseudocode for the dispatcher loop: getPending -> dispatch to projection handler -> projection updates read model -> markDispatched. Plus a note on idempotency in the projection handler.
B) Or a new docs/guide/projections.md page with the same content + a QueryHandler that reads from the projected table.

Either way include:
- Pseudocode for the dispatcher (polling-loop pattern; mention queue-based alternative)
- Projection handler shape (eventType-keyed map; idempotent updates)
- QueryHandler reading from the projection table
- One sentence on eventual-consistency lag

Reference implementation not needed — the pseudocode is the doc.
close_reason: Closed

title: Document snapshot policies (when, not just how)
description:
docs/guide/event-sourcing.md covers createSnapshot / restoreFromSnapshot mechanics but never addresses WHEN to snapshot. For event-sourced aggregates with thousands of events per stream, the snapshot policy dominates load time — and consumers will either over-engineer or skip the topic.

Three canonical strategies to cover:
1. **Every-N-events**: snapshot after every N pendingEvents flushed (e.g. N=100). Simple, predictable; can leave hot streams under-snapshotted if low volume.
2. **Time-based**: snapshot after T hours since last snapshot. Smooths bursts but wastes work on inactive streams.
3. **On-demand / background job**: dedicated worker that walks streams over a threshold and snapshots. Best at scale; requires more infrastructure.

Acceptance:
- New 'Snapshot policies' subsection in docs/guide/event-sourcing.md (~40 lines)
- Three strategies described with trade-offs
- Explicit statement: 'The library deliberately does not ship a SnapshotPolicy port — every store has different snapshotting facilities (Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY, EventStoreDB built-in, etc.). The aggregate exposes the primitives; the consumer wires the policy.'
- Half a page of doc, no code.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add Process Manager / Saga example (Order -> Payment -> Shipping)
description:
The library has all the building blocks (CommandBus, EventBus, Outbox, withCommit) but no example or doc shows how to wire them into a Vernon IDDD §12 process manager / saga.

Consumers will hit this immediately for any multi-step business flow (checkout, multi-aggregate workflows, distributed transactions via choreography).

Acceptance:
- New examples/saga/ directory
- Use case: Order placed -> Payment requested -> Payment received -> Shipping requested -> Shipped
- One aggregate per step (Order, Payment, Shipping) — or single Saga aggregate that tracks state, depending on which reads cleaner
- Wired via EventBus: each completed step's event triggers the next CommandBus dispatch
- Demonstrates compensating actions on failure (cancellation flow)
- Tests showing the happy path + one failure/compensation path

Explicitly do NOT ship a Saga abstraction. The library's stance is 'no machinery you don't need' — a clear example is more valuable than a half-baked port. Vernon §12-13 is reference material.

Cross-link the example from docs/guide/cqrs-and-buses.md and possibly its own short doc page 'docs/guide/sagas.md' that points at the example.
close_reason: Closed

title: Document Repository.delete + domain-event pipeline interaction
description:
src/repo/repository.ts IRepository.delete(id) is pure persistence — it does not integrate with the withCommit event pipeline. Consumers who want to emit an AggregateDeleted event atomically with the row removal currently have no documented path.

The canonical DDD options are:
1. Soft-delete via state mutation: agg.archive() records an AggregateArchived event, then repo.save(agg) flushes events + persists the state change. delete() is never called. Hard-deletion happens later via a separate GC / retention process.
2. Hard-delete with event harvest: inside withCommit's transactional callback, load agg, call agg.recordDeleted() (records AggregateDeleted event), then call repo.delete(agg.id) — and pass the aggregate to withCommit's aggregates array so the event flows through outbox before the row is gone.

Neither option is documented in docs/guide/repository.md or anywhere else. Consumers will reinvent.

Acceptance:
- New section in docs/guide/repository.md titled 'Deletion and Domain Events' (~30 lines)
- Covers: delete() is pure persistence; soft-delete recommended; hard-delete-with-event recipe
- Cross-link from outbox.md if relevant
close_reason: Closed

title: Reduce markPersisted footgun in Repository.save implementations
description:
src/repo/repository.ts IRepository.save returns Promise<void>. Implementors are responsible for calling aggregate.markPersisted(newVersion) after the write succeeds — to push the persisted version back into the aggregate and clear pendingEvents/domainEvents.

Problem: nothing at compile time enforces this. A typical first implementation forgets the call. Symptoms only show up at the second save: the aggregate's pendingEvents still hold the previously-flushed events, so the same domain events get added to the outbox a second time. Silent data-correctness bug.

Fix candidates (pick one or combine):

A) Document the requirement HARDER in IRepository.save JSDoc — include a 'common mistake' callout. Cheapest, but doesn't prevent the bug.

B) Ship a 'commitAggregate(repo, aggregate)' helper that wraps repo.save() + markPersisted() in the right order. Encourages users to use the helper instead of calling save() directly.

C) Change IRepository.save signature to return Promise<TAgg> (the post-persist aggregate, with markPersisted already applied). Library-side the helper would call markPersisted on the implementor's behalf. Cleanest API but requires implementors to return the aggregate from save() — more BREAKING.

D) Add a typed Result wrapper that the impl uses to declare success WITH the new version, and the library applies markPersisted: 'return repo.persisted(aggregate, newVersion)'.

Recommendation: B as additive (no breaking change), reserve C for a later major if uptake of B is good. Open question: do we even want to keep markPersisted public, or hide it behind a friend-access pattern that only the library helper can invoke?
close_reason: Design 2 applied per Vernon/Axon/EventFlow research: withCommit owns the post-save lifecycle (harvest pendingEvents inside tx, markPersisted post-commit, then publish). Repository.save is pure persistence — must NOT call markPersisted. New use-case return shape is { result, aggregates: [...] }. Eliminates both the markPersisted footgun AND the latent outbox-empty bug in the previous documented pattern.

title: Ship InMemoryOutbox reference implementation
description:
The library exports EventBusImpl as the in-memory reference implementation of EventBus, but Outbox<Evt> has no parallel impl shipped. Every test and example writes the same Map-backed boilerplate — see src/events/outbox.test.ts:12 (createInMemoryOutbox helper) and the docs at docs/guide/outbox.md.

Fix: extract the canonical in-memory shape into 'class InMemoryOutbox<Evt extends AnyDomainEvent> implements Outbox<Evt>' alongside EventBusImpl. Use eventId as dispatchId (standard choice). Export from src/index.ts.

Tests: migrate src/events/outbox.test.ts to use the shipped class instead of its local helper.

Bonus: docs/guide/outbox.md currently shows the helper inline as if every consumer should write their own. After this bead it can say 'for a quick start use InMemoryOutbox; for production back it with your transactional store'.
close_reason: Closed

title: Unify domainEvents/pendingEvents API across aggregate flavours
description:
The two aggregate flavours have parallel-but-inconsistent surfaces for the same concept (un-flushed events on the aggregate):

AggregateRoot (state-stored):
- domainEvents: ReadonlyArray<TEvent>
- clearDomainEvents(): void
- (no hasX / getCount / getLatest helpers)

EventSourcedAggregate (event-sourced):
- pendingEvents: ReadonlyArray<TEvent>
- clearPendingEvents(): void
- hasPendingEvents(): boolean
- getEventCount(): number
- getLatestEvent(): TEvent | undefined

Problem: any caller writing generic 'save any aggregate' code (typical Repository.save implementation) has to branch on the aggregate flavour to discover its un-flushed events. The IAggregateRoot interface doesn't expose either accessor.

Fix options:
1) Add a common accessor on IAggregateRoot (e.g. 'unpublishedEvents' / 'clearUnpublishedEvents'). Both flavours implement it. Existing names stay as deprecated aliases or get renamed outright (BREAKING).
2) Pick one terminology (probably 'pendingEvents' — more accurate for event-sourcing, generic enough for state-stored). Rename both flavours to use the same name. BREAKING.
3) Just align the helper surface: add hasUnpublishedEvents() / getEventCount() / getLatestEvent() to AggregateRoot, keep both naming idioms.

Recommendation: option 1 or 2 with full alignment. Option 3 is half-measure. BREAKING is acceptable — we are still in rc.x.
close_reason: Unified to pendingEvents/clearPendingEvents on both aggregate flavours. Hoisted to IAggregateRoot<TId, TEvent = never>. Dropped hasPendingEvents/getEventCount/getLatestEvent helpers — all trivial array-wrappers, replaced with .length / .at(-1) idioms. addDomainEvent kept (verb-object pattern describes type, container name describes lifecycle state — mixed convention is honest).

title: restoreFromSnapshot variants don't deep-clone the snapshot input
description:
src/aggregate/aggregate-root.ts:341 (AggregateRoot.restoreFromSnapshot) and src/aggregate/event-sourced-aggregate.ts:325 (EventSourcedAggregate.restoreFromSnapshotWithEvents) both do 'this._state = freezeShallow(snapshot.state)' without cloning. createSnapshot() correctly uses structuredClone(this._state) on the way OUT, but neither restore path clones on the way IN — the aggregate now shares structural ownership with the caller's snapshot object.

Footgun: a caller that mutates snapshot.state.someNestedChild.value after passing it to restoreFromSnapshot will bleed the mutation into the live aggregate (freezeShallow only freezes the top object). Asymmetric with createSnapshot, where the contract is clearly 'caller owns the result'.

Fix: 'this._state = freezeShallow(structuredClone(snapshot.state))' in BOTH restore methods. Add tests that mutate a nested field on the original snapshot post-restore and assert the aggregate is unaffected.

Note structuredClone cost: snapshots are not in a hot path; this is the same call already made in createSnapshot. No perf concern.
close_reason: Closed

title: Reconcile loadFromHistory version semantics with autoVersionBump flag
description:
src/aggregate/event-sourced-aggregate.ts has a documented contradiction between two JSDoc blocks:

- loadFromHistory (line 271) and restoreFromSnapshotWithEvents (line 339) unconditionally set 'startVersion + history.length' — the JSDoc explicitly documents this as the contract ('Version advances additively').
- autoVersionBump: false (EventSourcedAggregateConfig JSDoc) is documented as 'your event store assigns version numbers itself and you want the aggregate to track them via bumpVersion() / setVersion() calls instead' — implying the user controls versioning end-to-end.

These two promises contradict each other when autoVersionBump=false: the user thinks they own versioning, but loadFromHistory writes a derived version anyway. Two paths to fix:

A) Honor the flag: when autoVersionBump=false, skip the trailing setVersion(startVersion + history.length) in both loadFromHistory and restoreFromSnapshotWithEvents. Document that the user must set the version themselves after replay (or via the events' own version field).

B) Document the actual behavior: tighten the autoVersionBump JSDoc to clarify that the flag controls apply() ONLY — replay always derives version from history.length regardless. The current JSDoc is misleading; the test coverage at event-sourced-aggregate.test.ts:167 confirms apply() honors the flag but doesn't probe replay behavior in that mode.

Decision: figure out which interpretation matches actual user intent (likely A — if you want event-store-managed versions, you want THEM during replay too, not the kit overwriting them). Then implement that path + add tests for the autoVersionBump=false + loadFromHistory combination.
close_reason: Option A applied per DDD-expert research: removed EventSourcedAggregateConfig + autoVersionBump flag + bumpVersion method from EventSourcedAggregate. apply() always bumps version. Replay behavior unchanged (version = startVersion + history.length, the canonical rule). AggregateRoot's flag intentionally left untouched — that one is Vernon-conformant and has legitimate use cases.

title: Tighten EventBus/Outbox/withCommit constraints to DomainEvent
description:
The library's domain-event ports currently accept any { type: string } shape, looser than the concrete EventBusImpl which already constrains to DomainEvent<string, unknown>. Three call sites are inconsistent:

- src/events/ports.ts:31 — EventBus<Evt extends { type: string }>
- src/events/ports.ts:151 — Outbox<Evt> (no constraint at all)
- src/app/handler.ts:45 — withCommit<Evt extends { type: string }, ...>
- src/events/event-bus.ts:26 — EventBusImpl<Evt extends DomainEvent<string, unknown>> (already strict, the canonical shape)

This is a DDD-purity issue: the library is named 'DDD kit', the types are named 'DomainEvent', but the ports leak any tagged union through. Align everything to extends DomainEvent<string, unknown>.

BREAKING: existing tests using ad-hoc shapes like 'type TestEvent = { type: "OrderCreated"; orderId: string }' need to migrate to proper DomainEvent<...>. Specifically src/app/handler.test.ts uses such ad-hoc shapes throughout — those mocks need to be rebuilt with createDomainEvent or full DomainEvent literals. Same for any other test files that publish/outbox 'fake' events.

Acceptance:
1. EventBus, Outbox, withCommit constraints all extend DomainEvent<string, unknown>.
2. All tests pass after migration.
3. Public type re-exports unchanged (DomainEvent already exported).
4. CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased] BREAKING.
notes: Scope addition: also export a type alias 'AnyDomainEvent = DomainEvent<string, unknown>' from src/aggregate/domain-event.ts and use it in the three constraint sites (EventBus, Outbox, withCommit) and the Handler type from bead cic. Pure readability improvement — semantically identical to the inlined form. Re-export from src/index.ts if other DomainEvent types are re-exported there.
close_reason: Closed

title: JSDoc: clarify IQueryableRepository.find returns the full set (no pagination)
description:
Architecture review finding (BG-04 / BG-05, Gregg).

src/repo/repository.ts:115 — IQueryableRepository.find(filter) returns unbounded TAgg[]. Adding findPage(filter, { limit, cursor }) would force a pagination semantic (cursor vs offset vs page token) on every consumer — out of scope for a generic repo interface.

Fix (doc only): extend JSDoc on IQueryableRepository.find — 'Returns ALL aggregates matching the filter. For unbounded result sets, prefer a read-side projection (CQRS) or declare a domain-specific paged method on your concrete repository. The library does not prescribe pagination semantics.'

title: README: document recommended event-upcasting pattern (no port)
description:
Architecture review finding (GY-16..18, Young).

src/aggregate/domain-event.ts:125 — DomainEvent.version field exists, but no upcasting/migration hook is provided. Shipping an EventUpcaster port would be premature framework-building: real upcasting strategies (sync vs async, versioned chain, schema registry, dispatch-time vs replay-time) vary too much by domain.

Fix (doc only): add a README section 'Event versioning and upcasting' that documents the recommended pattern — 'wrap loadFromHistory with your upcasting layer; the library deliberately does not ship a port until usage patterns mature. Use event.version to dispatch to the right transform.' Include a short code sketch.

title: Document Outbox.add idempotency expectation on eventId
description:
Architecture review finding (GH-11, Hohpe).

src/events/ports.ts:151 — Outbox.markDispatched is explicitly documented as idempotent on already-marked ids. Outbox.add is not. Real outbox implementations will need a UNIQUE constraint on eventId to be safe under transactional retry inside withCommit — but the contract does not say so today.

title: README: AggregateRoot section should state behaviour-first expectation
description:
Architecture review finding (MF-12, Fowler — Tell, don't ask).

The 'state' getter on Entity/AggregateRoot must be public so invariant logic can reach the state (DDD needs read access for guards). But the README does not say explicitly that consumers should expose behaviour-rich methods on their aggregates rather than letting callers read the state and act on it externally.

Fix: add a short paragraph to the AggregateRoot section of README.md — 'Subclasses should expose intention-revealing methods (order.confirm(), order.cancel(...)) and treat state-access as an internal concern. Reaching into aggregate.state from a use case is a smell.'

title: Document shallow-freeze caveat on Entity.state getter
description:
Architecture review finding (EE-05, Evans).

src/entity/entity.ts:132 — The 'state' getter exposes _state, which was frozen via freezeShallow. The caveat (top-level writes throw in strict mode; nested objects remain mutable) lives in the freezeShallow JSDoc (entity.ts:200) but consumers read the getter, not the helper.

Fix: add a one-line note to the JSDoc on the public 'state' getter (and on IEntity.state) — 'shallow freeze only — nested object/array mutations bypass the freeze; treat returned state as read-only by convention'.

title: Backfill test gaps from audit
description: WHY: Audit surfaced multiple untested code paths and behavioral edges that need coverage to lock in pre-1.0 behavior. WHAT: Add tests for: (a) id.ts (currently zero coverage); (b) EventSourcedAggregate.apply() partial-mutation on handler throw; (c) loadFromHistory version drift after snapshot; (d) restoreFromSnapshotWithEvents mid-stream failure rollback; (e) domainEvents/pendingEvents/Entity.state mutation-leak via getter; (f) EventBus.subscribe same handler twice; (g) EventBus.once() never-fires path; (h) vo({nested}) freezes caller's nested reference; (i) deepOmit with __proto__/constructor keys and built-ins aliased not copied; (j) deepEqual symmetric cycles, Error comparison, class-vs-plain-object with same keys.

title: Document/handle Entity.validateState ctor-before-field-init footgun
description: WHY: entity/entity.ts:142-149 calls validateState in the constructor before subclass field initializers run. Subclasses that override validateState and reference their own fields will see undefined, a subtle JS class-initialization footgun. WHAT: Either move validation to a post-construction hook, or add a loud JSDoc warning on validateState plus a note in the README.
close_reason: JSDoc warning + pinning test added. validateState must be pure with respect to this; use only the state argument. No runtime change needed — rule is now visible to consumers

title: Bind IdGenerator brand at creation, not call site
description: WHY: core/id.ts:3 declares IdGenerator.next<T extends string>() => Id<T>, so the caller picks T and defeats the brand: gen.next() returns Id<string> and any caller can claim it as Id<'UserId'> for free. WHAT: Bind T at generator creation (e.g. createIdGenerator<'UserId'>() returns IdGenerator<'UserId'>) so next() returns the bound brand.

title: Make guard() generic in error type, return Result<void, E>
description: WHY: core/guard.ts:19 returns Result<true, string>. The literal true value is useless to callers and the idiomatic return is Result<void, E>. Locking E = string forecloses domain-specific error types. WHAT: Change signature to guard<E = string>(cond: boolean, error: E): Result<void, E>.

title: Expand Outbox port with markDispatched + getPending
description: WHY: events/ports.ts:79 Outbox<Evt> only exposes add(), which is insufficient to actually implement the outbox pattern. WHAT: Add getPending() and markDispatched(eventIds) methods; the latter depends on eventId being part of DomainEvent.

title: Use Array instead of Set in EventBusImpl so duplicate subscribe is called twice
description: WHY: event-bus.ts:30,41 stores handlers in Set<EventHandler<any>>, which silently coalesces if the same handler subscribes twice. Most pub/sub users expect subscribe-twice-call-twice semantics. WHAT: Switch the handler collection to Array, or document the coalescing behavior explicitly.

title: Define EventBus.publish ordering/parallelism contract
description: WHY: event-bus.ts:61 mixes sequential for...of across event types with Promise.allSettled parallel-within-type. Error collection at L82-87 means later events still publish before the throw, so callers see aggregate failure only after side-effects. WHAT: Decide and document the contract explicitly (e.g. events dispatched in order, handlers within a type run in parallel, partial failure reported via AggregateError after all handlers settle).

title: Add AbortSignal/timeout + reject path to EventBus.once()
description: WHY: events/event-bus.ts:52-59 has no timeout, no AbortSignal, no reject path; if the event never fires the promise leaks forever. This is a common footgun in tests. WHAT: Add an optional { signal?: AbortSignal, timeoutMs?: number } parameter to once() and reject the promise on either trigger.

title: Document/enforce domain-event ordering (record-after-mutation)

Vernon recommends 'record after state change'. WHAT: Either lint/enforce the convention, or add a prominent README and JSDoc note pointing subclasses to the record-after-mutation pattern.

title: Default AggregateRoot.TEvent to never; align autoVersionBump defaults

WHAT: Default TEvent to never to force explicit typing and align (or loudly document) the autoVersionBump defaults across both classes.

title: Fix ValueObject side-effects: deep-clone before freeze + symbol-aware freeze
description: WHY: value-object/value-object.ts:67 does shallow spread then deepFreeze, so the caller's nested objects are frozen in place as a side-effect, surprising users. Additionally, deepFreeze (L34) uses getOwnPropertyNames (string-only) while deepEqual covers symbols, an asymmetry between freeze and equality. WHAT: Deep-clone props before freezing (or document the side-effect) and include symbol keys in deepFreeze.
close_reason: vo() deep-clones via structuredClone before freezing — caller's nested objects no longer frozen as side-effect; deepFreeze iterates Reflect.ownKeys so symbol-keyed properties also freeze. Function-property edge case re-framed as 'reject — anti-pattern'

title: Replace isBuiltInObject heuristic with explicit tag allow-list
description: WHY: utils/array/is-built-in.ts:41 uses globalThis[name] === constructor which fails across realms (iframe/worker/vm), defeating the cross-realm robustness deep-equal.ts:115 was designed for. The proto !== Object.prototype heuristic (L46) misclassifies user classes as built-ins. WHAT: Drop both heuristics and use an explicit allow-list of Symbol.toStringTag values.
close_reason: Tag-only allow-list replaces globalThis-lookup and proto heuristic. Cross-realm safe; user classes with built-in-like names no longer misclassified

title: Fix deepOmit correctness: cycle cache, built-in copy, proto pollution
description: WHY: utils/array/deep-omit.ts cycle cache uses cached !== undefined (L62), misclassifying a legitimately-cached undefined. Built-ins return original reference (L85), contradicting deep-copy JSDoc. Object.create(getPrototypeOf(obj)) (L89) with bracket-assignment of arbitrary keys can write to __proto__/constructor for parsed-JSON input. Class instances skip constructor invariants. ignoreKeys.includes(key) should be a Set. WHAT: Use visited.has(obj), null-proto clone or dangerous-key skip, document comparison-only use, and Set lookup.
close_reason: cycle cache via .has(), built-ins cloned by type (Date/RegExp/Map/Set/structuredClone fallback), __proto__/constructor pollution defeated by Object.defineProperty, class-instance reconstruction documented, ignoreKeys probed via Set

title: Fix deepEqual correctness gaps: cycles, symbols, any-leak
description: WHY: utils/array/deep-equal.ts:74-79 keys only objA in the WeakMap, so symmetric cycles (A-B paired with different counterparts) misjudge. Symbol-key check at L208 is O(n^2): getOwnPropertySymbols(objB).includes(key) inside a loop. (arrA as any)[i] at L110 leaks any. WHAT: Use WeakMap<obj, WeakSet<obj>> for cycle tracking, build a Set of symbol keys once, and type via ArrayLike<number>.
close_reason: Cycle cache now pair-set (WeakMap<obj, WeakSet<obj>>); symbol-key membership O(1) via Set; any-leak in TypedArray branch removed via typed cast. 5 new tests added

title: Unify identity equality across aggregate/entity/value-object
description: WHY: sameVersion (aggregate.ts:99) uses === while sameEntity (entity.ts:194) uses deepEqual. Object-shaped IDs break ===, primitive IDs make deepEqual wasteful. ValueObject.equals is constructor-aware but deepEqual (utils/array/deep-equal.ts:118) is prototype-blind, so two different definitions of value identity ship in the same library. WHAT: Pick one identity strategy and apply consistently across aggregate, entity, and value-object modules.
close_reason: All entity-helper id comparisons now use === (was deepEqual). Aligns with sameVersion and branded Id<Tag> as the canonical id shape. ValueObject.equals deliberately keeps deep equality — it's pattern-specific

title: Direct _state mutation bypasses validateState()
description:
## Problem

`AggregateRoot._state` ist `protected` und direkt mutierbar. Das JSDoc-Beispiel in `aggregate-root.ts:96-105` empfiehlt sogar dieses Pattern:

```typescript
confirm(): void {
  this._state = { ...this._state, status: "confirmed" };
  this.bumpVersion();
}
```

Das bypassed `setState()` und damit `validateState()`. Invarianten werden nicht geprüft.

Evans (Blue Book, Chapter 6): *"Invariants, which are consistency rules that must be maintained whenever data changes, will involve relationships between members of the Aggregate."*

### Das Problem ist zweifach

1. **`_state` direkt setzen** umgeht Validierung — Developer müssen wissen, dass sie `setState()` nutzen sollen, aber das Toolkit zeigt das Gegenteil
2. **`setState()` ist optional** — es gibt keinen Mechanismus, der Invariant-Enforcement erzwingt

## Lösungsoptionen

**Option A: JSDoc-Beispiele fixen (minimal)**
Alle Beispiele auf `this.setState(...)` umstellen. `_state` bleibt `protected` für Edge Cases, aber die empfohlene API ist `setState()`.

**Option B: `_state` auf `private` setzen**
Nur `setState()` als Zugang. Erzwingt Validierung. Breaking Change für Subclasses, die `_state` direkt nutzen.

**Option C: Getter/Setter mit Validation**
```typescript
protected set state(newState: TState) {
  this.validateState(newState);
  this._state = newState;
}
```

## Empfehlung

Option A als Quick-Fix für den RC, Option B als Follow-up für 1.0.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] JSDoc-Beispiele in `AggregateRoot` und `Entity` nutzen `setState()` statt direktem `_state` Zugriff
- [ ] Dokumentation macht klar, dass `setState()` der empfohlene Weg ist
- [ ] Optional: `_state` auf `private` setzen (Breaking Change bewusst entscheiden)
close_reason: Closed

title: EventBus.subscribe: eventType should be Evt["type"] instead of string
description:
## Problem

In `src/events/ports.ts:58` und `src/events/event-bus.ts:32` akzeptiert `subscribe` einen `eventType: string`:

```typescript
subscribe: <T extends Evt>(
  eventType: string,        // ← hier geht Typsicherheit verloren
  handler: EventHandler<T>,
) => () => void;
```

Das bedeutet: Consumer können beliebige Strings übergeben, auch solche, die keinem tatsächlichen Event-Typ entsprechen. Der Compiler kann illegale Event-Namen nicht ablehnen.

### Beispiel

```typescript
type OrderEvent =
  | DomainEvent<"OrderCreated", { orderId: string }>
  | DomainEvent<"OrderShipped", { trackingId: string }>;

const bus = new EventBusImpl<OrderEvent>();

// Kompiliert heute – sollte es aber nicht:
bus.subscribe("OrderCancelled", handler);  // Typo/falscher Event-Typ
```

## Vorgeschlagene Lösung

```typescript
subscribe: <T extends Evt>(
  eventType: T["type"],     // ← Compiler prüft gegen die Event-Union
  handler: EventHandler<T>,
) => () => void;
```

Alternativ, falls `T["type"]` die Inference nicht richtig einschränkt:

```typescript
subscribe: <K extends Evt["type"]>(
  eventType: K,
  handler: EventHandler<Extract<Evt, { type: K }>>,
) => () => void;
```

Gleiches gilt für `once()` in ports.ts und event-bus.ts.

## Betroffene Stellen

- `src/events/ports.ts` – Interface `EventBus.subscribe` und `EventBus.once`
- `src/events/event-bus.ts` – `EventBusImpl.subscribe` und `EventBusImpl.once`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `subscribe` und `once` akzeptieren nur gültige Event-Type-Strings
- [ ] Ungültige Event-Typ-Strings erzeugen einen Compile-Error
- [ ] Bestehende Tests passen sich an (sollten bereits korrekte Strings nutzen)
- [ ] Neuer Test: Type-Level-Test der den Compile-Error bei falschem String belegt (z.B. via `// @ts-expect-error`)
close_reason: Closed
