title: Update README: replace BETA WARNING with RC notice
description:
## Problem

Im README.md (Zeile 5-7) steht aktuell:

```markdown
> **⚠️ BETA WARNING**
>
> This library is currently in beta. The API is subject to change until a stable 1.0.0 release. Breaking changes may occur in minor versions during the beta phase. Please pin your dependencies to specific versions.
```

Wenn der RC auf npm veröffentlicht wird, ist diese Warnung irreführend. Ein RC signalisiert API-Stabilität mit dem Ziel eines 1.0.0 Release – das Gegenteil von "subject to change".

## Fix

BETA WARNING durch eine RC-angemessene Notice ersetzen, z.B.:

```markdown
> **Release Candidate**
>
> This library is in Release Candidate phase. The API is considered stable and ready for production evaluation. Please report any issues before the final 1.0.0 release.
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] BETA WARNING Block im README durch RC Notice ersetzen
- [ ] Kein Verweis auf "beta" mehr im README
- [ ] Muss zeitgleich mit dem RC-Version-Bump passieren (nicht vorher)
close_reason: Closed

title: Evaluate: typed error channel for CommandBus/QueryBus (Result<R, string> -> Result<R, E>)
description: GATE FIRST: evaluation, not a committed feature. CommandBus.execute and QueryBus.execute currently return Result<R, string> (src/app/command-bus.ts, query-bus.ts): the error channel is a bare string, so handlers lose error type information at the bus boundary and callers cannot discriminate failures without parsing strings. A generic error type (Result<R, E>, defaulting E=string for backward shape, or threading E through the TMap) would let consumers carry DomainError/typed unions across the bus. This is best done in a MAJOR (it widens a core return type), so it was deliberately deferred out of 2.0 rather than rushed in. Decide WHETHER to do it: weigh (1) real consumer pain from stringly-typed bus errors vs (2) the kit's minimalism and the fact that throwing DomainError + catching at the App boundary already covers typed errors (Result vs Throw guide). If the throw-axis already serves typed errors well, 'wontfix' is a legitimate outcome. If built: design the generic signature, default, TMap interaction, and migration; target the next major.
notes: DECISION: built option A. The error channel is now generic E (default string), additive and backward compatible; widened E requires an errorMapper at construction (type-enforced), default describeThrown for string. Aligns the implementation with the documented Result<T,E> App-boundary contract. Files: command.ts (CommandHandler<C,R,E=string>), command-bus.ts + query-bus.ts (E param, CommandBusOptions/QueryBusOptions, errorMapper, conditional constructor args). 7 new tests, docs + CHANGELOG updated.
close_reason: Built A: generic Result<T,E> error channel for CommandBus/QueryBus (default string, errorMapper for typed E). Aligns impl with the documented contract. 711 tests green.

title: Ship a reference retrying TransactionScope with OCC/serialization classification + backoff
description: The UoW design relies on pairing it with a retrying TransactionScope for serialization-failure/OCC retries, but the kit ships none, so every consumer hand-rolls retry-with-backoff (error classification, jitter, cap) and gets it wrong. Ship a reference retrying scope wrapper that classifies retryable failures (ConcurrencyConflictError, serialization failures via the cause-chain helpers) and applies bounded backoff with jitter. Rounds out the ultra-robust story.
close_reason: RetryingTransactionScope: someChainRetryable classification, exp backoff +/-20% jitter, last-error-unchanged on exhaustion, AbortSignal-bounded waits, constructor validation; outbox-must-be-transactional documented; shared abortReason helper extracted; code-review fixes folded in; 13 tests

title: Split CommitError into retryable (transient) vs deterministic (programming-bug) classes
description: CommitError currently folds together a transient commit-time serialization failure (retryable) and a deterministic harvest-guard violation (missing aggregateId/aggregateType, pre-set aggregateVersion ahead of commit version: programming bugs). A caller that blindly retries CommitError will infinite-loop on the deterministic cases. The docstring says inspect the cause and retryable helpers exist, but a distinct non-retryable error class for the harvest-guard failures would make the right thing structural instead of advisory.
close_reason: EventHarvestError (BaseError-direct, non-retryable) for harvest-guard violations; UoW passes it through via cause-chain walk (wrapping-scope safe); CommitError narrowed to transient outbox/commit; code-review fixes folded in; 5 tests

title: decide(repo): DuplicateAggregateError / constraint-violation mapping
description: Checklist review 2026-06-11 §12: an INSERT hitting a duplicate key currently surfaces as a raw driver error — not catchable by class, not mapped to the kit's taxonomy. Decide: ship DuplicateAggregateError (InfrastructureError; thrown by consumer repos on unique-violation, the same delegation model as ConcurrencyConflictError) + document the mapping per driver (Postgres 23505, MySQL 1062, SQLite UNIQUE), or document why raw propagation is acceptable. If shipped, add to the repository guide's save() skeleton (insert path) and to the contract test suite (insert same id twice → DuplicateAggregateError).
notes: Post-decision review round (9 findings, all fixed): duplicate built at version 2 + snapshotState comparison so a clobbering insert is detectable (the untouched-row assertion was vacuous at equal versions); new semantic opt-out insertsAreDuplicateChecked (default TRUE — save() is insert-or-update, never upsert; create-idempotency belongs in the use case) so deliberately upserting adapters skip ONLY the duplicate test without losing deletion-finality coverage, with the skip named after the right capability; result-vs-throw.md (the central HTTP-mapping guide) updated (class listing, 409 mapping with opposite retry semantics, user-message list); isUniqueViolation documented as a consumer-written driver predicate with ORM-wrapping hint; InfrastructureError JSDoc subclass list completed; createAggregateWithId JSDoc names both gated tests; unit-of-work.md coverage list + LLM.md taxonomy grouping (CommitError/RollbackError ARE InfrastructureError) and testing-entry annotations fixed.
close_reason: DECIDED: ship it. DuplicateAggregateError (InfrastructureError, pinned name, not retryable, user-safe message without id leak) — same delegation model as ConcurrencyConflictError: kit ships class + per-driver mapping docs (PG 23505, MySQL 1062, SQLite SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_UNIQUE), consumer repo throws on the insert path. Repository guide save() skeleton shows the try/catch mapping; contract suite gains a capability-gated duplicate-insert test (existing row untouched) + mutant test; in-memory reference implements the check; LLM.md stale 'only concrete classes' line fixed. 6 new tests.

title: evaluate(uow): read-only UoW mode — decide IF before building (wontfix is a valid outcome)
description:
GATE FIRST: this bead is an EVALUATION, not a committed feature. Step 1 is deciding WHETHER to build at all — 'evaluated, decided against, closed as wontfix' is a fully legitimate outcome. Do not start implementation before the evaluation is recorded here.

Evaluation criteria (go only if ALL hold): (1) a real consumer presents a concrete use case that plain reads cannot serve — note the kit's own architecture already answers most read needs WITHOUT a UoW: the CQRS read side / projections guide covers query paths, and write-side getById inside a normal run() covers read-for-decision; (2) the use case genuinely needs UoW machinery on a read path (identity-map consistency across multiple loads in one operation) rather than just a query; (3) the added API surface (read-only registry typing, runtime save/delete throws, a second run-mode) is justified against the kit's minimal-surface philosophy. If any criterion fails: close with the rationale.

IF built (original sketch, requirements doc §17): uow.read(work) — no write transaction unless the DB requires one, save/delete unavailable (compile-time via a read-only registry type, plus runtime throw), no markPersisted, identity map optionally active for consistency.

title: Consolidate clone walkers and built-in taxonomy (cloneForVo vs deepOmit; 3 tag sets; entity contract docs)
description: Maintenance cluster from code review: (1) cloneForVo re-implements deepOmit's walker incl. verbatim assignOwn copy while ValueObject uses deepOmit — two cloners with divergent semantics in one module; (2) built-in taxonomy exists in 3 hand-maintained copies (BUILT_IN_TAGS in is-built-in.ts, SNAPSHOT_SAFE_TAGS in base-aggregate.ts, cloneBuiltIn's alias set in deep-omit.ts) — export one classification and derive; (3) Entity's class-instance pass-through contract is documented only on the private shallowCopyOwned helper — surface it on the public ctor/setState docs. Found by code review of fix branch.
close_reason: Done: shared REFERENCE_COMPARED_TAGS, entity contract docs public. Walker unification deliberately NOT done: cloneForVo (ownership clone) and deepOmit (comparison tool with aliasing semantics) now have documented, intentionally divergent leaf semantics — unifying them would couple two different contracts

title: shadowMutators allocates 16 closures + defineProperty per Date on the event hot path
description: src/value-object/value-object.ts:52 — createDomainEvent deep-freezes every event incl. occurredAt, paying 16 closure allocations + 16 defineProperty calls per event minimum. Fix: module-level shared frozen thrower functions (message built lazily inside the throw, keyed by a lookup) eliminate per-instance closure allocation. Found by code review of fix branch.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/p3-cleanups; 5 new tests

title: deepFreeze mutator deny-list fails open for future runtime mutators (Map.getOrInsert)
description: src/value-object/value-object.ts:24 — deny-by-enumeration: only listed mutator names throw. Stage-3 upsert proposal (Map.prototype.getOrInsert/getOrInsertComputed) ships in engines; on such a runtime vo({m:new Map()}).m.getOrInsert('k',v) silently inserts into a deeply-immutable VO after a Node upgrade with zero code change. Consider fail-closed design (shadow all own-prototype functions except reader allowlist) or document the limitation. Found by code review of fix branch.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/p3-cleanups; 5 new tests

title: voWithValidation/voValidated throw instead of returning Result on edge-case inputs
description: src/value-object/value-object.ts:176 and src/validation/vo-validated.ts:43 — (1) error path: the default error message does JSON.stringify(t), which throws on cyclic or BigInt-containing input (TypeError: Converting circular structure to JSON) instead of returning err — only when no custom errorMessage is given. (2) success path: vo() uses structuredClone, which throws DataCloneError for values containing functions — after validation already passed — instead of returning a Result. Both violate the Result-based contract.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/p3-cleanups; 5 new tests

title: Non-Error throws degrade to '[object Object]' without cause across buses
description: src/app/command-bus.ts:162-164, src/app/query-bus.ts:169-171, src/events/event-bus.ts:138 — catch paths do error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error) (or new Error(String(reason))). A handler throwing/rejecting with a non-Error object (e.g. { code: 'DB_CONN' }, common from driver SDKs) yields err('[object Object]') / Error('[object Object]') with no cause attached — all diagnostic information destroyed. Fix: attach the original value as cause (new Error(String(reason), { cause: reason })) and consider preserving stacks.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/p3-cleanups; 7 new tests

title: InMemoryOutbox.getPending: negative limit returns all-but-last instead of empty
description: src/events/outbox.ts:51 — all.slice(0, limit) with limit = -1 returns everything except the last pending record (slice end-relative indexing). A dispatcher computing a limit dynamically (e.g. batchSize - inFlight going negative) silently dispatches almost the whole backlog instead of nothing. Trigger: outbox.add([e1,e2,e3]); await outbox.getPending(-1) → [e1, e2]. Fix: clamp limit to >= 0.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/p3-cleanups; 7 new tests

title: deepEqual: NaN handling inconsistent across Float64Array, invalid Date, Number wrapper
description: src/utils/array/deep-equal.ts:124, 178, 191 — deepEqual(NaN, NaN) === true for primitives, but: deepEqual(new Float64Array([NaN]), new Float64Array([NaN])) → false (element compare uses !==); deepEqual(new Date(NaN), new Date(NaN)) → false (getTime() is NaN); deepEqual(new Number(NaN), new Number(NaN)) → false (valueOf() === valueOf()). Use Object.is or explicit NaN checks for these paths to match documented primitive NaN semantics.
close_reason: Fixed: sameValueZero in TypedArray/Date/wrapper comparisons; 3 new tests

title: withCommit: markPersisted loop lacks error isolation, enabling event double-emission
description: src/app/handler.ts:145-147 — the post-commit loop for (const agg of aggregates) { agg.markPersisted(...) } has no error isolation. markPersisted calls the user-overridable onPersisted hook (base-aggregate.ts:165-168); if the first aggregate's hook throws, the remaining aggregates keep stale pendingEvents — the next withCommit touching them re-harvests and re-adds those events to the outbox (double emission, the failure mode base-aggregate.ts:152-156 warns about), bus.publish is skipped entirely, and the committed call still rejects.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/p3-cleanups; 7 new tests

title: CommandBus/QueryBus: duplicate registration silently overwrites previous handler
description: src/app/command-bus.ts:143 and src/app/query-bus.ts:153 — this.handlers.set(type, ...) replaces an existing entry with no warning, error, or log. Two modules each registering a handler for 'CreateOrder' silently route all commands to whichever registered last; the first handler is dead code with no signal. Tests only cover multiple distinct types. Consider throwing or warning on duplicate registration.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/p3-cleanups; 7 new tests

title: loadFromHistory([]) sets persistedVersion=0, flipping INSERT routing to UPDATE
description: src/aggregate/event-sourced-aggregate.ts:161 — markRestored(startVersion + 0) runs unconditionally on success, so a fresh aggregate (persistedVersion undefined = never-persisted sentinel, which per base-aggregate.ts:65-70 routes INSERT vs UPDATE) that loads an empty stream ends with persistedVersion = 0 — a subsequent save() issues an UPDATE against a row that does not exist. The failure-path test (event-sourced-aggregate.test.ts:852-870) asserts the sentinel must survive precisely so a follow-up save() routes to INSERT, but the empty-success path violates the same invariant and is untested.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/p3-cleanups; 2 new tests

title: mergeMetadata: Object.assign triggers __proto__ setter (prototype pollution)
description: src/aggregate/domain-event.ts:473 — Object.assign uses [[Set]], which invokes the __proto__ setter: mergeMetadata(JSON.parse('{"__proto__":{"isAdmin":true}}')) yields an object with zero own keys whose metadata.isAdmin reads true via the injected prototype. Metadata frequently round-trips through JSON (outbox rows, message envelopes), making attacker-influenced keys plausible. copyMetadata (line 451) is safe because spread uses [[DefineOwnProperty]] — only mergeMetadata is affected.
close_reason: Fixed on fix/p3-cleanups; 2 new tests

title: llms.txt: reorder Common Mistakes by failure-mode category
description:
Mini-polish to the 10-entry Common Mistakes section. Currently roughly frequency-ordered; reorder into three categories for stricter scannability:

1. Compile-time errors (TS yells immediately): missing TEvent generic, pre-rc.6 names, wrong save() shape, events-vs-aggregates return.
2. Silent runtime bugs (passes type-check, fails in production): markPersisted-override-without-super, markPersisted-inside-save, factory globals across parallel tests, missing identity map, module-level aggregate on Workers.
3. Architectural / testing mistakes: mocking the bus instead of using it.

Within each bucket keep the original wording — only reorder. Headings can be inline emphasis (no new subheadings) so the section stays compact.

User flagged as optional / non-blocking; doing it because the reordered version is materially easier to scan and the cost is one Edit.
close_reason: Closed

title: Document DI-vs-globals trade-off for Clock/EventId factories in design-decisions.md
description:
Vernon IDDD §13's preferred pattern for Clock and IdFactory is constructor injection on aggregates. The kit defaults to module-globals + per-call override + scoped helpers (set/withEventIdFactory, set/withClockFactory). Reading the current docs in order, a consumer sees the global-with-helpers as THE supported path — Vernon-DI is invisible.

The truth: the kit ALLOWS Vernon-DI without any library change. createDomainEvent already accepts per-call { eventId, occurredAt }; a consumer can ignore the globals entirely, thread factories through aggregate constructors, and use per-call options at every recording site. This is Vernon-pure and works today.

The doc gap: design-decisions.md doesn't surface this as an architectural choice. Without it, Vernon-leaning readers either assume the kit endorses globals as best practice, or roll their own DI ad-hoc without realising the kit's per-call override is the canonical hook for it.

Acceptance:
1. New short section in docs/guide/design-decisions.md naming the Globals-vs-DI trade-off. Position: after the existing 'Identity ids are branded strings, generated app-side' section (the natural home — that section already establishes the per-call-options philosophy for ids).

2. Section content:
   - Acknowledge Vernon §13's DI-canonical position
   - Trade-off table (Globals: minimal aggregate constructors, default fast path, scoped helpers for tests | DI: race-free structurally, explicit deps, heavier construction surface)
   - Show that Vernon-DI works on top of the existing API with no library change — short code snippet of Order with constructor-injected clock + idGen, using createDomainEvent's per-call options.eventId / options.occurredAt
   - State the kit's stance plainly: defaults exist for simplicity (95% production case: emit events with default clock + UUID); Vernon-DI is fully supported on top for research- or test-heavy codebases that need it.

3. No code change. Doc-only.

4. CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased] as Documentation, non-breaking.
close_reason: Closed

title: Document IdGenerator collision and monotonicity requirements
description:
src/core/id.ts IdGenerator<Tag> interface is generic over the id-source. The default eventId factory uses crypto.randomUUID (UUIDv4) which is collision-resistant. But consumers supplying their own IdGenerator may pick weak sources:

- Date.now() alone — duplicates under concurrent calls within the same millisecond
- Sequential counter without persistence — resets on process restart, collides with prior runs
- Custom logic without proper entropy

The JSDoc on IdGenerator / id.ts says nothing about these requirements. Consumers can implement an IdGenerator that compiles cleanly but produces duplicates under load.

Acceptance:
1. JSDoc note in src/core/id.ts on the IdGenerator interface stating: 'Your factory must produce unique ids under concurrent calls. crypto.randomUUID (default), ULID, UUIDv7, KSUID all satisfy this; Date.now() alone or a non-persistent counter do not.'
2. Brief mention of the same point in docs/guide/getting-started.md or docs/guide/domain-events.md (wherever id-generation is first introduced) — single sentence, no separate section needed.
3. No code change. Doc-only.
4. CHANGELOG entry as Documentation under [Unreleased].
close_reason: Closed

title: Duplicate-aggregate guard in withCommit
description:
If a use case accidentally passes the same aggregate instance twice in the aggregates array (withCommit({ ... }, async () => ({ result, aggregates: [order, order] })) the harvest currently produces:

- Events double-harvested: aggregates.flatMap returns each event twice
- Outbox receives the same events twice (duplicate dispatchId entries if InMemoryOutbox; row-uniqueness collision if a SQL outbox)
- markPersisted called twice on the same aggregate (second call is a no-op since pendingEvents is empty, but version assignment runs twice with the same value)

Concrete cause patterns: use case touches the same aggregate via two repository references that happen to resolve to the same identity-map entry; explicit return of aggregates: [agg1, agg2] without checking agg1 !== agg2; refactor accidents.

Fix: dedupe aggregates by reference inside withCommit before harvest. Use Array.from(new Set(fnResult.aggregates)). Set-by-reference uses JS object-identity. Will not catch the harder case (two different instances with the same id() value) — that's a separate Aggregate-Instance-Sharing violation and the kit cannot reasonably detect it at this layer.

Acceptance:
1. withCommit dedupes the aggregates array by object identity before harvest.
2. Regression test: pass [order, order] and assert outbox sees the events once, markPersisted called once on order.
3. JSDoc on withCommit mentions the dedupe behaviour briefly so the contract is explicit.
4. CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased] as Changed: defensive behaviour change, non-breaking — no consumer's existing code does worse after this change.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add onPersisted Template-Method hook; protect markPersisted from accidental override
description:
Real-world consumer feedback: a Restaurant aggregate overrode markPersisted without calling super.markPersisted(version). Effect: parent's pendingEvents = [] reset never ran, so subsequent withCommit harvests re-dispatched stale events through the outbox. The bug is in user code (forgotten super call) but the library's API surface invited it — markPersisted is public on IAggregateRoot, it is the only obvious lifecycle hook on the aggregate, and there is no 'post-persist customisation point' next to it. Users who want to log / audit / evict caches on persist reach for markPersisted because it is the only method that fires at that moment. Forgetting super silently corrupts the outbox-dispatch lifecycle.

Fix: GoF Template Method pattern. Framework owns the must-always-run step (setVersion + clear pendingEvents); user code overrides a separate protected hook called onPersisted(version) that fires AFTER the cleanup.

Acceptance:

1. AggregateRoot and EventSourcedAggregate both expose protected onPersisted(version: Version) with a no-op default body.
2. Their markPersisted implementations call this.onPersisted(version) after the setVersion + pendingEvents reset.
3. JSDoc on markPersisted updated with a framework-lifecycle warning: 'Framework lifecycle method. Subclasses should override onPersisted(version) instead; if you override markPersisted directly, you MUST call super.markPersisted(version) first to preserve the pending-events cleanup.'
4. Test: a subclass overrides onPersisted, verifies it gets called with the right version, and pendingEvents is empty by the time onPersisted runs.
5. Test: a subclass that overrides markPersisted without calling super still breaks the pendingEvents cleanup — a negative test documenting exactly the footgun the new hook helps avoid.
6. CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased] as Added, non-breaking — the new override point ships without changing existing behaviour for users who don't reach for it.
notes: Re-classified from P2 to P3 after consumer-side review: the bug is in user code (forgotten super.markPersisted call), not a library regression. The fix is structural hardening — closing the API-surface footgun that invites the override — not a defect repair. Target rc.7 or post-1.0 minor release; let the design bake before adding a new public override point. Immediate workaround for affected consumers: call super.markPersisted(version) first inside any markPersisted override.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add Bounded-Context note to design-decisions.md
description:
The library is one TypeScript module — it doesn't enforce or model bounded-context boundaries. That's intentional (BCs are a higher-level architectural concern), but consumers reading the API will wonder how multi-BC apps should be structured.

A one-sentence answer prophylactically closes the question.

Acceptance:
- One short paragraph in docs/guide/design-decisions.md (under an existing section if appropriate, or its own 'Bounded Contexts' heading)
- Content: 'ddd-kit is bounded-context-agnostic. Each bounded context is a module/package (or even its own repo) that imports the kit; the kit does not prescribe layout, naming, or inter-BC integration. Inter-BC communication is typically via published domain events through the outbox + a message broker — exactly the topology the kit is designed for, but enforcing nothing.'
- 4-5 lines max
close_reason: Closed

title: Add Domain Services note to design-decisions.md
description:
Vernon IDDD §7 — Domain Services hold business logic that doesn't naturally belong to an aggregate or value object (e.g. 'compute exchange rate', 'check inventory across multiple aggregates'). The library deliberately ships no marker interface or class for them — they're just functions or interfaces in consumer code.

This is the right call (no abstraction is the right abstraction here), but consumers reading the API surface will wonder where Domain Services fit. A one-paragraph note in docs/guide/design-decisions.md would close the question prophylactically.

Acceptance:
- New short section 'Domain Services' in docs/guide/design-decisions.md (~10 lines)
- States: 'Domain Services are consumer constructs. The kit does not ship a marker interface because the only thing one would do is identify them — and the file naming or module structure already does that. A Domain Service in this kit is just a function or interface defined alongside your aggregates.'
- Includes a 5-line code snippet: 'function calculateShippingCost(order: Order, address: Address): Money'
close_reason: Closed

title: Document event-ordering contract across aggregates in withCommit
description:
src/app/handler.ts:66-72 harvests events via aggregates.flatMap(a => a.pendingEvents). The actual ordering contract:
1. Aggregates returned in the order they appear in the use-case fn's returned 'aggregates' array
2. Within each aggregate, events are in emission order (pendingEvents preserves insertion order)

So the resulting outbox/bus event order is: agg[0].events..., agg[1].events..., etc.

Subscribers WILL come to rely on this ordering — particularly for sagas where 'OrderConfirmed' must arrive before 'PaymentRequested'. Currently the contract is implicit in the implementation.

Acceptance:
- Add one sentence to the 'Order of operations' list in docs/guide/outbox.md describing the harvest order
- Add a JSDoc note to withCommit in src/app/handler.ts stating the same
- Optional: add a test asserting the order (cheap insurance against accidental regression)
close_reason: Closed

title: Document static-factory convention for aggregate construction
description:
Every example in the kit uses static factory methods for aggregate construction:
  - examples/order/order.ts:23 -> Order.create(id, customerId)
  - docs/guide/aggregates.md:38 -> static draft() / static create()
  - docs/guide/event-sourcing.md:30 -> static create()

But the prose never states this as the recommended pattern. Vernon IDDD §11 calls this 'Aggregate Factory'. The convention exists in all examples; it just isn't surfaced.

Two related points to cover:
1. Use static factory methods (not public constructors) for creation. Reasons: encapsulate validation, record creation events atomically, give the operation a domain name ('Order.place(...)' is clearer than 'new Order(...)').
2. The creation event (e.g. OrderCreated) is recorded inside the factory method. The library does NOT auto-emit a creation event — that's the consumer's call.

Acceptance:
- Half a paragraph near the top of docs/guide/aggregates.md naming the convention
- A 2-line code snippet showing 'Order.create(...)' calling 'this.addDomainEvent({ type: "OrderCreated", ... })' inside the factory
- Mention that protected constructors prevent direct 'new Order(...)' from outside the module
close_reason: Closed

title: Add 'Where invariants live' map to aggregates guide
description:
DDD invariants can live in four different places, each appropriate for a different kind of rule:

1. **Per-state (validateState)**: structural invariants ('total >= 0', 'items.length <= 100'). Runs on every setState call.
2. **Per-event (validateEvent on EventSourcedAggregate)**: pre-mutation guards against applying an event in an invalid state ('OrderShipped only after OrderConfirmed').
3. **Per-method (in domain methods like confirm(), ship())**: business-rule guards that throw DomainError on violation before any state change.
4. **Cross-aggregate (saga / process manager)**: invariants that span multiple aggregates can only be coordinated eventually-consistently via events + sagas.

These four locations exist today in the library and examples but are nowhere collected. Consumers re-derive this map every time and often choose the wrong location.

Acceptance:
- New subsection 'Where invariants live' in docs/guide/aggregates.md (~30 lines)
- Maps each rule shape to its location with a one-line example
- Explicit warning: cross-aggregate consistency is eventual; if you need transactional cross-aggregate invariants, you have the wrong aggregate boundaries (Vernon §10)
close_reason: Closed

title: Add dedicated tests for errors.ts, scope.ts, sameVersion
description:
Three small but real test-coverage gaps surfaced during the library audit:

1) src/core/errors.ts — no dedicated test file. Coverage is incidental via handler.test.ts and repository.test.ts (AggregateNotFoundError, ConcurrencyConflictError). What's NOT directly tested:
   - InfrastructureError and DomainError base behavior (timestamp, cause-chain propagation through .cause assignment, withUserMessage, toJSON shape)
   - MissingHandlerError
   - The retryable hint on ConcurrencyConflictError end-to-end through @shirudo/base-error's isRetryable predicate (partially covered in repository.test.ts but not in errors.test.ts itself)

2) src/repo/scope.ts — no dedicated test. TransactionScope is currently exercised only via handler.test.ts (transactional ordering). The interface itself is small but documenting the expected contract in a dedicated test would help downstream implementors (Drizzle/Prisma/Mongo) sanity-check their impls against a reference.

3) src/aggregate/aggregate.ts sameVersion() — exported helper at line 56. No grep hits in any test file. Quick test: two aggregates with same id+version → true; same id different versions → false; different ids → false.

Acceptance:
- src/core/errors.test.ts created with cases above
- src/repo/scope.test.ts created (or a TransactionScope-specific block extracted from handler.test.ts)
- src/aggregate/aggregate.test.ts gets a sameVersion describe block (if file exists; otherwise create)
- All tests pass; total count grows from 395 to ~410+
close_reason: Added dedicated tests for src/core/errors.ts (15 tests covering DomainError/InfrastructureError base behavior, MissingHandlerError hierarchy guarantee, AggregateNotFoundError user-message hiding ids, ConcurrencyConflictError retryable hint through wrapped cause chain) and src/repo/scope.ts (5 tests covering tx-handle threading, return/error propagation, no-ctx idiom, Drizzle-flavoured impl smoke). Third audit item (sameVersion) was a false positive — already tested in src/aggregate/aggregate.test.ts:414.

title: Widen entity collection helpers to ReadonlyArray<T>
description:
src/entity/entity.ts collection helpers declare T[] as input but never mutate:

- findEntityById<TId, T>(entities: T[], id: TId): T | undefined  (line 259)
- hasEntityId<TId, T>(entities: T[], id: TId): boolean  (line 283)
- removeEntityById<TId, T>(entities: T[], id: TId): T[]  (line 309)

Plus any sibling helpers (filterEntities, mapEntities, findEntityIndex — check the full file).

These helpers are designed to be called on the entities-array of a frozen aggregate state — but a 'readonly OrderItem[]' is not assignable to 'OrderItem[]', forcing callers to cast or copy. The input is consumed read-only in every helper; widen to 'ReadonlyArray<T>'.

Return types stay as T[] (the helpers create fresh arrays in removeEntityById; findEntityById returns single elements anyway).

Non-breaking for callers (T[] is assignable to ReadonlyArray<T>); fixes the broken case where the caller already holds ReadonlyArray<T>.
close_reason: Closed

title: Use ReadonlyArray for loadFromHistory event input
description:
src/aggregate/event-sourced-aggregate.ts:35 (in IEventSourcedAggregate) declares 'loadFromHistory(history: TEvent[]): Result<void, DomainError>'. The mutable TEvent[] signature suggests the library might mutate the caller's event stream — it doesn't. Switch to ReadonlyArray<TEvent> to express the contract: we only consume, never write back.

Tighten the same way in the concrete EventSourcedAggregate class implementation if there's a separate method signature. Check whether replay() or any sibling method has the same issue.

Non-breaking for callers (mutable arrays are assignable to ReadonlyArray<T> parameters). May require minor adjustments inside the implementation if it indexes/iterates in a way TS flags (unlikely — for-of and index access are fine on ReadonlyArray).

Acceptance:
1. loadFromHistory(history: ReadonlyArray<TEvent>) in interface AND implementation.
2. Audit replay() and similar methods for same pattern.
3. Tests pass.
close_reason: Closed

title: Constrain Handler<TState, TEvent> to DomainEvent in event-sourced-aggregate
description:
src/aggregate/event-sourced-aggregate.ts:58 declares 'type Handler<TState, TEvent> = (state: TState, event: TEvent) => TState;' without constraining TEvent. The type is only used inside EventSourcedAggregate — the broader aggregate class already requires 'TEvent extends DomainEvent<string, unknown>'. Add the same constraint to the Handler alias so a misuse (e.g. handlers keyed on non-event types) becomes a compile error rather than relying on the parent class's constraint to catch it indirectly.

Non-breaking: internal type alias, no public surface change. All existing handlers already satisfy the DomainEvent constraint via the surrounding class.

Acceptance:
1. Handler<TState, TEvent extends DomainEvent<string, unknown>>.
2. Tests pass without changes.
3. No CHANGELOG entry needed (internal tightening).
close_reason: Closed

title: Tighten EventBusImpl handlers map from any to Evt
description: src/events/event-bus.ts:30 currently has 'private readonly handlers = new Map<string, EventHandler<any>[]>();' with an eslint-disable. The class is already generic over 'Evt extends DomainEvent<string, unknown>', so the storage type can be EventHandler<Evt>[] instead of EventHandler<any>[]. The cast in subscribe() at line 41 ('handler as EventHandler<Evt>') stays because of function-parameter contravariance (EventHandler<Extract<Evt,{type:K}>> is not assignable to EventHandler<Evt>), but the storage type becomes honest and the eslint-disable can be removed.
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix TEvent JSDoc default in AggregateRoot
description: src/aggregate/aggregate-root.ts:105 JSDoc claims '@template TEvent - The type of domain events recorded by this aggregate (defaults to unknown)' but the actual default is TEvent = never (declared at line 124). Update the doc to reflect the real default and explain the rationale: never means 'aggregates without recorded events allow no event type' — emitting any event without a TEvent generic is a compile error, which is the safer default.
close_reason: Closed

title: sameAggregate() has misleading semantics — checks version, not identity
close_reason: Closed

title: Add tests for guard utility function
description:
## Problem

`src/core/guard.ts` ist die einzige exportierte Utility-Funktion ohne eigene Tests. Die Funktion ist trivial (ein Einzeiler), aber für eine vollständige Test-Abdeckung der Public API sollte sie getestet sein.

```typescript
export function guard(cond: boolean, error: string): Result<true, string> {
  return cond ? ok(true) : err(error);
}
```

## Scope

Minimale Tests:
- `guard(true, "error")` → `ok(true)`

title: evaluate(uow): auto-flush — decide IF before building (wontfix is the likely outcome)
description:
GATE FIRST: this bead is an EVALUATION, not a committed feature. Step 1 is deciding WHETHER to build at all — 'evaluated, decided against, closed as wontfix' is a fully legitimate (and per the kit's philosophy, LIKELY) outcome. Do not start implementation before the evaluation is recorded here.

Evaluation criteria (go only if ALL hold): (1) demonstrated consumer PAIN with explicit save — not pattern-completeness ('Fowler's UoW has flush') and not aesthetics; concretely: repeated production bugs from forgotten save() calls that tests did NOT catch, in more than one consumer codebase; (2) the existing mitigations are shown insufficient — hasChanges makes redundant saves free no-ops, forgotten saves surface in tests (the documented §24 stance: explicit behavior over hidden magic), and enrollment already moved the footgun from per-call-site to per-repository; (3) the magic cost is accepted: auto-flush hides WHEN writes happen, complicates debugging, and couples flush order to the kit. The docs publicly classify the current design as 'a transaction coordinator with registration and Identity Map — auto-flush is a designed, optional later phase, to be built only against proven need': honoring that bar is the point of this gate. If any criterion fails: close with the rationale.

IF built (original design notes preserved): loaded aggregates tracked automatically (identity map IS the tracking set); new aggregates registered explicitly or via repo methods; deleted aggregates registered explicitly; deterministic flush order (see MikroORM CommitOrderCalculator notes on the epic); hasChanges === false skipped (safe since the 2026-06-11 hardening: version delta, pendingEvents, keyless fallback); markPersisted only after commit; concurrency errors never hidden; no event publish before commit. Possible API: mutations inside run() without explicit save().
notes: MikroORM blueprints if auto-flush is ever built: CommitOrderCalculator (~1650-1687) computes a TYPE-LEVEL topological order from FK dependencies — our deterministic-flush-order requirement; without relation metadata, the order spec must come from the registry (e.g. registration order or an explicit ordering hint). Flush sequence worth copying: DELETE_EARLY (reverse order) → CREATE (forward) → UPDATE (forward) → DELETE (reverse) (~1322-1373). computeChangeSets() walks the identity map to find dirty managed entities (~725-796) — our equivalent walk is trivial: identity map entries where hasChanges === true. Orphan-remove stack + recompute-during-hook patterns only matter if flush hooks ever exist.

title: Investigate tightening CommandBus/QueryBus handlers map
description: src/app/command-bus.ts:120 and src/app/query-bus.ts:132 both store handlers as Map<string, ...Handler<any, any>> with eslint-disable. Unlike the EventBus case, a direct switch to a more constrained type runs into input-contravariance issues: each handler accepts only its specific Command/Query subtype, not the union Command/Query. Any tighter storage type (e.g. Handler<Command, unknown>) requires a cast at register() so the savings are marginal — moves the 'any' to a cast site. Decision: investigate whether there's a cleaner approach (branded internal type, never-input trick) that genuinely improves type safety. If not, document the rationale next to the eslint-disable and close as won't-fix.
close_reason: Option A (closure adapter) applied to both CommandBus and QueryBus. Storage type is now StoredCommandHandler / StoredQueryHandler (concrete Function shape, no any). The dispatch-invariant cast moves into a documented closure body — single, well-justified narrowing site. Removed both eslint-disable comments.
